style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Satisfying requirements via the Drag and Drop handler in a view</h4><p class="auto-cursor-target">To satisfy a requirement via the Drag and Drop handler in a view</p><hr /><ul><li>Drag and drop a requirement element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Drag and Drop handler" /></ac:link> and drop it on:<ul><li data-uuid="9be157a6-fe22-49e6-8f4f-e6ce3c852075">a part element symbol that you want to satisfy the requirement by referencing it using the <strong>Create Satisfy Requirement</strong> command.</li><li data-uuid="810ae531-54b3-4994-8ca9-c5b38d934b3b"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">a satisfy requirement usage symbol that you want to satisfy</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">by referencing it </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">using the</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> <strong>Set Satisfied Requirement</strong><strong> </strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">command.</span></li></ul></li></ul><h4><span>Satisfying requirements via the Specification panel</span></h4><p>To satisfy a requirement via the Specification panel</p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="1d846dab-a105-4660-902a-c1b73b20dd91">Drag and drop a requirement element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Drag and Drop handler" /></ac:link> and drop it on the<span> <strong>Satisfied Requirement</strong></span><strong><span> </span></strong>field in the Properties section of the Specification panel for the satisfy requirement usage. <span><br /></span>The appropriate requirement is specified for the element.</li></ul><h4>Satisfying requirements via the view palette</h4><p><span>To satisfy requirements via the view palette</span></p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="58464135-2e5d-4daf-a0a0-6e7cbf7a9bd1">In the view palette, under the Requirements group, click <strong>satisfy</strong>.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b6722e1c-23c5-4540-bc91-9847171bc871"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to the<span> <strong>satisfy requirement </strong></span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="8aeea71d-c713-48d1-bcd7-391d43970b82">Do one of the following:<ol><li><span>To satisfy an existing requirement, in the view pane, click on the part element symbol, then on the requirement element symbol. </span></li><li><span>To satisfy a new requirement, in the view pane, click on the part element symbol, then elsewhere in the view pane to create a requirement element symbol. <br /></span>The <strong>satisfy</strong><span> path is drawn between the element symbols.</span></li></ol></li></ol><h4><span>Satisfying requirements via the smart manipulator in a view</span></h4><p><span>To satisfy requirements via the smart manipulator in a view</span></p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="bb455742-5303-4682-a884-2dd3578b09b7">In a<span> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">view</span>, do one of the following:<ul><li><span>To satisfy an existing requirement, </span>select the part element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the<strong><span> satisfy </span></strong>command. Click on the requirement element symbol.</li><li><span>To create a new satisfy requirement, </span>select the part element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the<strong><span> satisfy </span></strong>command. Click <span>elsewhere in the view pane to create a requirement element symbol</span>.<br />The <strong>satisfy</strong><span> path is drawn between the element symbols.</span></li></ul></li></ul><h4><span>Satisfying requirements </span><span>via the Create Relation command</span></h4><p>To satisfy requirements via the Create Relation command</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="8c6bfd10-86f5-4363-8bb6-8c798c6e495f">Right-click on the client (source) element.</li><li data-uuid="c888fff2-f206-4d7b-86a6-a9cd53c7f423">In the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Relation</strong>, then in the submenu, under <strong>Outgoing</strong>, click <strong>satisfy</strong>. The <strong>Create... </strong>dialog opens.</li><li data-uuid="32eb8246-106a-4a22-a627-36ccc6afa040">In the <strong>Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, do one of the following:<ol><li>In a view, click the element symbol you want to be the supplier (target). Hold Shift to select multiple elements.</li><li>Click the <strong>Add <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong>button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to be the supplier (target) and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Drag and drop the needed element you want to be the supplier (target) from the Containment tree onto the <strong>Create... </strong>dialog.</li></ol></li><li data-uuid="1ebd27f3-6c73-4ce4-a99a-fbe3faca6864">In the <strong>Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, click <strong>OK</strong>. </li></ol><h3>Satisfying a requirement<span> </span><span>via the owned </span>satisfy requirement<span> </span><span>usage </span><span>itself </span><span>as the </span><span>satisfied requirement</span></h3><h4><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d1743b7e-ed24-41e9-8b0d-8ef738c0b766"><ac:parameter ac:name="">Satisfying requirements via the Textual Editor2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Satisfying requirements via the Textual Editor</h4><p class="auto-cursor-target">To satisfy a requirement via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor within the curly braces of the part element and do one of the following:<ol><li>To specify a requirement without the satisfying feature, declare the <em>satisfy requirement </em>keyword followed by the qualified name of the requirement the part satisfies. </li><li>To specify a requirement with the satisfying feature declare the <em>satisfy requirement </em>keyword followed by the qualified name of the satisfied requirement, then state the <em>by </em>keyword and the name of the subject element that satisfies the requirement. <ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="165cb277-93b5-4472-ab56-56acb347e9e1"><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target">To negate a satisfy requirement usage, specify the <em>not</em> keyword before the satisfy keyword, e.g., <em>not satisfy</em>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li><li><span>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.</span><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11412740-abbe-4304-92f9-b5eb0e976c7d"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">vehicle_c1</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Vehicle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span>satisfy requirement </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">vehicleSpecification</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// satisfied requirement is the defined satisfy requirement usage itself contained by the parent part that is the implicit satisfying feature</span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Satisfying requirements via the Create Element command</h4><p>To satisfy a requirement via the Create Element command</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Element</strong>.</p></li><li>In the <strong>Create Element</strong> dialog, select <strong>satisfy requirement</strong>.</li><li>Name the element.</li><li>(Optional) Specify the satisfy requirement.</li></ol><h4><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c907bd5b-cbe5-443b-ad9a-7cc27be2cea6"><ac:parameter ac:name="">Satisfying requirements via the view palette2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Satisfying requirements via the view palette</h4><p>To satisfy a requirement via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Requirements/Constraints group, click <strong>satisfy requirement</strong>.<strong> </strong></li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the element symbol.</li><li>(Optional) Specify the satisfy requirement.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577140 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00682: Saving as image

- page_id: `249577140`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577140/Saving+as+image
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-19T15:20:02.111+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model management > Working with views > View image export
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can save an image as an active diagram, selected symbols, or selected diagrams.

Diagrams and symbols that were created in the model can be saved as images in the following formats:

- Enhanced Metafile Format (*.emf)
- Encapsulated PostScript (*.eps)
- Joint Photographic Experts Group (*.jpg, *.jpeg)
- Portable Network Graphics (*.png)
- Scalable Vector Graphics (*.svg)
- Tagged Image File Format (*.tif, *.tiff)
- Windows Metafile Format (*.wmf)

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Save As Image dialog when the Selected diagrams option button is selected.

##### Saving an image

To save an image

1. Open the diagram you want to save as an image. If you want to save only particular symbols, select them on the diagram.
2. On the main menu, select File > Save as Image . The Save As Image dialog opens.
3. In the dialog:
  - Select whether to save an **Active diagram**, **Selected symbols**, or **Selected diagrams**. Selected diagrams**Selected diagrams** allow you to search for a particular diagram in your model.
  - Specify the directory you want to save the image to.
  - Select the image format. Click Options to specify image options according to the selected format.
4. Click Save . The image is saved in the directory you have specified.

##### Save As Image dialog elements

| Dialog element | Element type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Active diagram | Option button | Select to save the currently active diagram as an image. The active diagram name is displayed in parentheses next to the option button name. |
| Selected symbols | Option button | Select to save specific symbols that are selected in the diagram as an image. The option is active only if one or multiple symbols are selected in the diagram. |
| Selected diagrams | Option button | Select the option to save the diagrams selected in the diagram list as images. To select multiple diagrams, hold Shift and then select the needed diagrams with the click of the mouse. |
| Select diagrams | Text box | The text box is available only if the Selected diagrams option button is selected.In the Select diagrams box, type the name of the diagram you want to find. Search results are displayed as a list right under the text box. Select the diagram(s) you want to save as image(s).To optimize your search, use . |
| Image File / Working Directory | Text box | Select the working directory in which the image will be saved. |
| Image Format | Drop-down list | Select the image format. |
| Options | Button | Click to open the Properties dialog where you can specify image export properties, such as image resolution and exported image size. The properties provided in the Properties dialog depend on the selected image format. |
| Overwrite existing files | Check box | Select to overwrite already saved image files. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can save an image as an active diagram, selected symbols, or selected diagrams.</p><p>Diagrams and symbols that were created in the model can be saved as images in the following formats:</p><ul><li>Enhanced Metafile Format (*.emf)</li><li>Encapsulated PostScript (*.eps)</li><li>Joint Photographic Experts Group (*.jpg, *.jpeg)</li><li>Portable Network Graphics (*.png)</li><li>Scalable Vector Graphics (*.svg)</li><li>Tagged Image File Format (*.tif, *.tiff)</li><li>Windows Metafile Format (*.wmf)</li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="save_as_image_dialog.png" /></ac:image><br />The Save As Image dialog when the Selected diagrams option button is selected.</h6><h3>Saving an image</h3><p>To save an image</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the diagram you want to save as an image. If you want to save only particular symbols, select them on the diagram.</li><li>On the main menu, select <strong>File </strong>&gt; <strong>Save as Image</strong>. The <strong>Save As Image</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>In the dialog:<ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Select whether to save an <strong>Active diagram</strong>, <strong>Selected symbols</strong>, or <strong>Selected diagrams</strong>. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f4d6f1f2-7008-40ac-aa9b-912d4c92a548"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Selected diagrams</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Selected diagrams</strong> allow you to search for a particular diagram in your model.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Specify the directory you want to save the image to.</li><li>Select the image format. Click <strong>Options </strong>to specify image options according to the selected format.</li></ul></li><li>Click <strong>Save</strong>. The image is saved in the directory you have specified.</li></ol><h3>Save As Image dialog elements</h3><table><colgroup class=""><col class="" /><col class="" /><col class="" /></colgroup><tbody class=""><tr class=""><th>Dialog element</th><th colspan="1">Element type</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>Active diagram</strong></td><td colspan="1">Option button</td><td><p>Select to save the currently active diagram as an image. The active diagram name is displayed in parentheses next to the option button name.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>Selected symbols</strong></td><td colspan="1">Option button</td><td><p>Select to save specific symbols that are selected in the diagram as an image. The option is active only if one or multiple symbols are selected in the diagram.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><td><p><strong>Selected diagrams</strong></p></td><td colspan="1">Option button</td><td><p>Select the option to save the diagrams selected in the diagram list as images. To select multiple diagrams, hold Shift and then select the needed diagrams with the click of the mouse.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>Select diagrams</strong></td><td colspan="1">Text box</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>The text box is available only if the <strong>Selected diagrams</strong> option button is selected.</p><p>In the <strong>Select diagrams</strong> box, type the name of the diagram you want to find. Search results are displayed as a list right under the text box. Select the diagram(s) you want to save as image(s).</p><p>To optimize your search, use <ac:link ac:anchor="Quick Find dialog elements"><ri:page ri:content-title="Quick find" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[the search filters]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></div></td></tr><tr class=""><td><p><strong>Image File / Working Directory</strong></p></td><td colspan="1">Text box</td><td>Select the working directory in which the image will be saved.</td></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>Image Format</strong></td><td colspan="1">Drop-down list</td><td>Select the image format.</td></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>Options</strong></td><td colspan="1">Button</td><td><p>Click to open the <strong>Properties dialog</strong> where you can specify image export properties, such as image resolution and exported image size. The properties provided in the <strong>Properties</strong> dialog depend on the selected image format.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><td><p><strong>Overwrite existing files</strong></p></td><td colspan="1">Check box</td><td>Select to overwrite already saved image files.</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=314180957 space=SYSML2P version=8 -->
## PAGE 00683: Selecting SysML v1 models for migration

- page_id: `314180957`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/314180957/Selecting+SysML+v1+models+for+migration
- version_number: 8
- version_date: `2026-06-25T11:39:00.639+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > SysML v1 to SysML v2 transition > SysML v1 to v2 model conversion
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

##### Overview

This activity diagram describes how organizations identify and prepare SysML v1 projects for migration to SysML v2.

##### Process

The process begins by reviewing existing SysML v1 projects and selecting those that are relevant for migration.

In addition to selecting projects, organizations must determine which baselines or project versions should be migrated. Migrating the complete historical evolution of a project is often impractical due to the significant effort required for each migration.

To support this decision, the SysML v1 environment should be capable of automatically evaluating migration readiness by identifying unsupported model elements and estimating transformation feasibility.

Once a project is selected:

- The project is marked for migration.
- Migration readiness is assessed.
- Access to the source model may be restricted to prevent further modifications during migration preparation.

This helps ensure that the migration process is performed against a stable project baseline.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Selecting SysML v1 models to migrate.jpg" /></ac:image></strong></p><h3>Overview</h3><p>This activity diagram describes how organizations identify and prepare SysML v1 projects for migration to SysML v2.</p><h3>Process</h3><p>The process begins by reviewing existing SysML v1 projects and selecting those that are relevant for migration.</p><p>In addition to selecting projects, organizations must determine which baselines or project versions should be migrated. Migrating the complete historical evolution of a project is often impractical due to the significant effort required for each migration.</p><p>To support this decision, the SysML v1 environment should be capable of automatically evaluating migration readiness by identifying unsupported model elements and estimating transformation feasibility.</p><p>Once a project is selected:</p><ul><li data-uuid="3e9365ad-007f-4484-89f4-a3d463b41a8f">The project is marked for migration.</li><li data-uuid="8591a328-3231-400e-b9d2-6f9ff4ce81af">Migration readiness is assessed.</li><li data-uuid="82a27160-9d8f-4366-a3ca-5b4e00d6e450">Access to the source model may be restricted to prevent further modifications during migration preparation.</li></ul><p>This helps ensure that the migration process is performed against a stable project baseline.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577553 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00684: Send action

- page_id: `249577553`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577553/Send+action
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-19T15:36:27.434+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions > Transfers between actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A send action usage is declared as an action usage whose behavior is to transfer the payload from the sender to the receiver. It has three input parameters:

1. A set of payload values, identified by the send keyword.
2. A sender occurrence, identified by the via keyword.
3. A receiver occurrence, identified by the to keyword.

**Sending context**

A send action usage can specify both a sender (*via*) and receiver (*to*), but it will generally give only one or the other:

- If a send action usage is directly or indirectly a composite feature of a part definition or usage, then the default for the sender ( via ) of the send action usage is the containing part.
- If a send action usage is not a composite feature of a part definition or usage, then the default for the sender ( via ) of the send action usage is the highest-level containing action usage.
- If a send action usage is contained by a perform action, then the default for the sender ( via ) of the send action usage is the perform action, not the part or the higher-level action.
- If sending through a port, the port usage will usually be the sender ( via ). The receiver ( to ) is then determined by the port interface connection.

##### Specifying send actions for actions

###### Specifying send actions for actions via the Textual Editor

To specify send actions for actions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the send action and do one of the following:
  1. For a named send action usage, declare the keyword action , specify the element name, and then the keyword send .
  2. For an unnamed send action usage, declare the keyword send .
2. Follow with the expression for the payload.
3. Follow with the needed option(s):
  1. Specify the via keyword and the expression for the sender.
  2. Specify the to keyword and the expression for the receiver.
4. Click the Synchronize button.

// ------------ sending without ports; the sender is the part 'camera', the receiver is the part 'screen'

partcamera:Camera{ 
actiontakePicture:TakePicture{ 
actiontriggeracceptscene:Scene; 
thenactionfocus:Focus{ 
inscene=trigger.scene; 
outimage; 
} 
flowfocus.imagetoshoot.image; 
thenactionshoot:Shoot{ 
inimage; 
outpicture; 
} 
// named send action 'displayPicture' transferring the payload 'shoot.picture' from the sender 'camera' to the receiver 'screen': 
thenactiondisplayPicturesendshoot.picturetoscreen; 
// unnamed send action usage: 
 then send shoot.picture to screen; 
} 
}

// ------------ sending through ports; the sender is the port 'displayPort'

partcamera:Camera{ 
portviewPort; 
portdisplayPort;actiontakePicture:TakePicture{ 
actiontriggeracceptscene:SceneviaviewPort; 
thenactionfocus:Focus{ 
inscene=trigger.scene; 
outimage; 
} 
flowfocus.imagetoshoot.image; 
thenactionshoot:Shoot{ 
inimage; 
outpicture; 
} 
 // named send action 'displayPicture' transferring the payload 'shoot.picture' from the sender 'displayPort':thenactiondisplayPicturesendshoot.picturevia displayPort;// unnamed send action usage:then send shoot.picture via displayPort; 
} 
}

###### Specifying send actions for actions via the view palette

To specify send actions for actions via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Other Actions group, click the send button to create a send action usage element.
2. Click on an action symbol in the view pane where you want to create the send symbol.
3. (Optional) Specify the element's parameters via the Textual Editor.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">image</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">            </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">out</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">picture</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">        }<br />        <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// named send action '<span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">displayPicture</span>' transferring the payload '<span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">shoot</span>.<span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">picture</span>' from the sender '<span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">displayPort</span>':<br /></span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">        then</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">action</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">displayPicture</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">send</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">shoot</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">picture</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">via</span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">displayPort</span>; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">        // unnamed send action usage:<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><br />        <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">then</span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">send</span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">shoot</span>.<span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">picture</span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">via</span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">displayPort</span>; </span></span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Specifying send actions for actions via the view palette</h4><p>To specify send actions for actions via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Other Actions group, click the <strong>send </strong>button to create a send action usage element.</li><li>Click on an action symbol in the view pane where you want to create the send symbol.</li><li>(Optional)<span> </span>Specify the element's parameters via the Textual Editor.</li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=263094959 space=SYSML2P version=15 -->
## PAGE 00685: Sequence Functions

- page_id: `263094959`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/263094959/Sequence+Functions
- version_number: 15
- version_date: `2026-04-22T15:08:37.878+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model analysis > Functions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The table below lists the functions available in the*SequenceFunctions*namespace of the Standard Libraries.

- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 

| Function | Example | Example (Osvaldo) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| element at index positionabstract function # { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature index[1] : Positive} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; (x) # (y)}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute b = 2;attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) 20 | package IndexTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 5, 1); attribute indexTest = a#(3);}Output>> indexTest 5 | Returns the element at the specified index position in the sequence. Indexing starts at 1.Related: |
| equalsabstract function equals { in ref feature x[0..*] : Anything in ref feature y[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; equals(x, y) }attribute a = (10, 20);attribute b = (20, 10);attribute result = Example(a, b); Output>> result = Example(a, b) false | package EqualsTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a1 = (10, 20); attribute b1 = (20, 10); attribute c1 = (20, 10); attribute equalsTest1 = equals(a1, b1); attribute equalsTest2 = equals(b1, c1);}Output >> equalsTest1 false >> equalsTest2 true | Returns true if sequences x and y contain the same elements in the same order. |
| sameabstract function same { in ref feature x[0..*] : Anything in ref feature y[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; same(x, y) }attribute a = (10, 20);attribute b = (20, 10);attribute result = Example(a, b); Output>> result = Example(a, b) false | package SameTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a1 = (10, 20); attribute b1 = (20, 10); attribute sameTest1 = same(a1, b1); attribute sameTest2 = same(a1, a1); }Output >> sameTest1 false >> sameTest2 true | Returns true if sequences x and y refer to the same instance. |
| sizeabstract function size { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; size(x) }attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute result = Example(a);Output>> result = Example(a) 3 | package SizeTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (10, 20, 30); attribute sizeTest = size(a);}Output >> sizeTest 3 | Returns the number of elements in the sequence x.Related: |
| isEmptyabstract function isEmpty { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; isEmpty(x) }attribute a = ();attribute result = Example(a);Output>> result = Example(a) true | package IsEmptyTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (10, 20, 30); attribute isEmptyTest = isEmpty(a); }Output >> isEmptyTest false | Returns true if the sequence x has no elements.Related: |
| notEmptyabstract function notEmpty { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; notEmpty(x) }attribute a = (1, 2);attribute result = Example(a);Output>> result = Example(a) true | package NotEmptyTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (10, 20, 30); attribute notEmptyTest = notEmpty(a);}Output >> notEmptyTest true | Returns true if the sequence x contains one or more elements.Related: |
| includesabstract function includes { in ref feature seq1[0..*] : Anything in ref feature seq2[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; includes(x, y)}attribute a = (4, 2, 3);attribute b = (3, 2);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) true | package IncludesTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 3); attribute b = (3, 2); attribute includesTest = includes(a, b);}Output >> includesTest true | Returns true if sequence x contains elements in sequence y. |
| includesOnlyabstract function includesOnly { in ref feature seq1[0..*] : Anything in ref feature seq2[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; includesOnly(x, y)}attribute a = (4, 2, 3);attribute b = (3, 2);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) false | package IncludesOnlyTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 3); attribute b = (4, 3, 2); attribute includesOnlyTest = includesOnly(a, b);}Output >> includesOnlyTest true | Returns true if all elements of sequence x are included in sequence y. |
| excludesabstract function excludes { in ref feature seq1[0..*] : Anything in ref feature seq2[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; excludes(x, y)}attribute a = (2, 3);attribute b = (4);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) true | package ExcludesTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 3); attribute b = 1; attribute excludesTest = excludes(a, b);}Output >> excludesTest true | Returns true if none of the elements in sequence y are present in sequence x. |
| unionabstract function union { in ref feature seq1[0..*] : Anything in ref feature seq2[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; union(x, y)}attribute a = (2, 3);attribute b = (4, 3);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) 2 3 4 3 | package UnionTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 3); attribute b = 1; attribute unionTest = union(a, b);}Output >> unionTest 4 2 3 1 | Returns a new sequence containing all elements from sequences x and y. |
| intersectionabstract function intersection { in ref feature seq1[0..*] : Anything in ref feature seq2[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; intersection(x, y)}attribute a = (2, 3);attribute b = (1, 2);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) 2 | package IntersectionTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 3); attribute b = 2; attribute intersectionTest = intersection(a, b);}Output >> intersectionTest 2 | Returns a sequence containing elements common to both sequences x and y. |
| includingabstract function including { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature values[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; including(x, y)}attribute a = (2, 3);attribute b = 2;attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) 2 3 2 | package IncludingTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2); attribute b = 2; attribute includingTest = including(a, b); }Output >> includingTest 422 | Returns a new sequence with element y appended to the end of sequence x. |
| includingAtabstract function includingAt { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature values[0..*] : Anything in ref feature index[1] : Positive} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; in i; includingAt(x, y, i)}attribute a = (2, 3);attribute b = 1;attribute c = 2;attribute result = Example(a, b, c);Output>> result = Example(a, b, c) 2 1 3 | package IncludingAtTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2); attribute b = 5; attribute includingAtTest = includingAt(a, b, 2); }Output >> includingAtTest 4 5 2 | Returns a new sequence with element y inserted into sequence x at position i. |
| excludingabstract function excluding { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature values[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; excluding(x, y)}attribute a = (2, 3);attribute b = 2;attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) 3 | package ExcludingTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2); attribute b = 2; attribute excludingTest = excluding(a, b); }Output >> excludingTest 4 | Returns a new sequence with all occurrences of element y removed from sequence x. |
| excludingAtabstract function excludingAt { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature startIndex[1] : Positive in ref feature endIndex[1] : Positive} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; in z; excludingAt(x, y, z)}attribute a = (1, 2, 3, 4);attribute b = 2; // start indexattribute c = 3; // end indexattribute result = Example(a, b, c);Output>> result = Example(a, b, c) 1 4 | package ExcludingAtTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 5, 1); attribute b = 2; attribute excludingAtTest = excludingAt(a, b, 2);}Output >> excludingAtTest 4 5 1 | Returns a new sequence with elements removed from sequence x between indexes y and z. |
| subsequenceabstract function subsequence { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature startIndex[1] : Positive in ref feature endIndex[1] : Positive} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; in z; subsequence(x, y, z)}attribute a = (1, 2, 3, 4);attribute b = 1; attribute c = 3; attribute result = Example(a, b, c);Output>> result = Example(a, b, c) 1 23 | package SubsequenceTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 5, 1); attribute subSequenceTest = subsequence(a, 2, 3);}Output >> subSequenceTest 2 5 | Returns a new sequence containing elements from sequence x starting at y and ending at z. |
| headabstract function head { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; head(x)}attribute a = (1, 2, 3, 4);attribute result = Example(a);Output>> result = Example(a) 1 | package HeadTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 5, 1); attribute headTest = head(a); }Output >> headTest 4 | Returns the first element of sequence x.Related: |
| tailabstract function tail { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; tail(x)}attribute a = (1, 2, 3, 4);attribute result = Example(a); Output>> result = Example(a) 2 3 4 | package TailTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 5, 1); attribute tailTest = tail(a); }Output >> tailTest 2 5 1 | Returns a sequence containing all elements of x except the first.Related: |
| lastabstract function last { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; last(x)}attribute a = (1, 2, 3, 4);attribute result = Example(a);Output>> result = Example(a)4 | package LastTest { private import SequenceFunctions::*; attribute a = (4, 2, 5, 1); attribute lastTest = last(a);}Output >> lastTest 1 | Returns the last element of sequence x.Related: |

/>1</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"> </span></p></div></td><td><ul><li data-uuid="c8ab2811-6bc0-4c9d-8a51-06bae1ec847d">Returns the last element of sequence x.</li><li data-uuid="5edbea99-5737-4c56-bd17-0bd24520abf1">Related:<ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="last"><ri:page ri:content-title="Collection Functions" <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=314182402 space=SYSML2P version=6 -->
## PAGE 00686: Snapshot

- page_id: `314182402`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/314182402/Snapshot
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2026-07-01T14:56:49.921+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Common elements > Occurrences
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A snapshot is a type of suboccurrence. It is a time slice with zero duration, representing an occurrence at a specific point in time.

##### Specifying or creating elements as snapshots

###### Specifying or creating elements as snapshotsvia the Textual Editor

To specify or create elements as snapshots via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, declare the *snapshot* keyword prior to the declaration of the element that you want to specify as a snapshot.
2. Click the **Synchronize**button. snapshotpartcarInMotion;

###### Specifying elements as snapshots via the shortcut menu

To specify elements as snapshots via the shortcut menu

- Right-click a usage element in the Containment tree or its symbol in a view and in its shortcut menu, in the Properties group, click **Portion Kind**, and in its submenu, click **snapshot** .

###### Creating elements assnapshots via the view palette

To create elements as snapshots via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, do one of the following:
  1. To create an occurrence usage snapshot or one that will correspond to the element kind for which the snapshot will be created:
    1. Click the**snapshot** button. If it is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to the **timeslice** button and click the **snapshot** button in the dropdown menu.
    2. Click in the view pane or the element symbol for which you want the snapshot to be created:
      1. Creating an element on the empty space of the view pane creates an occurrence usage element as a snapshot by default.
      2. Creating an element on another element symbol creates a snapshot for it:
        1. If the element symbol is a definition, the created snapshot is also a usage (e.g. part) that corresponds to the definition element for which the snapshot is created (e.g. part def).
        2. If the element is a usage, the created snapshot is also a usage of the same kind as the element for which the snapshot is created.
  2. To specify the snapshot's element kind:
    1. Click the down-facing arrow next to the **timeslice** button, and in its dropdown menu, click **any snapshot...** and select the specific element kind you need.
    2. Click in the view pane or the element symbol for which you want to create the snapshot.

If a snapshot element is created for an element specified as an individual, the snapshot is also created as an individual by default.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577226 space=SYSML2P version=46 -->
## PAGE 00689: Specification panel

- page_id: `249577226`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577226/Specification+panel
- version_number: 46
- version_date: `2026-07-13T15:36:58.474+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Element management
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Specification panelallows you to review and modify properties of specific elements, such as the declared name, declared short name, feature type and value, documentation, and others. You can also review inherited features and modify or create new owned elements in the appropriate compartments, which are equivalent to those displayed in view symbols. To review all element properties, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] page.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Specification panel displays the *vehicleMassLimitationRequirement*element's specification information.

#### Working with the Specification panel

##### Opening the Specification panel

To open the Specification panel, do one of the following

- In the Textual Editor, right-click an element and in the shortcut menu, click Specification .
- Double-click an element in the Containment tree or its symbol in the View Pane.
- Select an element in the Containment tree or its symbol on the View Pane and press Enter.
- Right-click an element in the Containment tree or its symbol on the View Pane and click **Specification**in its shortcut menu.
- Select an element in the Containment tree or its symbol on the View Pane, then in the main menu, click **Window**> **Specification**.
- To open the Specification panel for a specific element displayed in the compartments section when the Specification panel is already open for another element, hover over the needed element and click the **Navigate [IMAGE alt='' src='']**button.
- Right-click an element cell in a tabular view and in its shortcut menu, click the **Specification**command. **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

##### Switching between Specification panel tabs

Opening the Specification panel opens the Properties tab by default.

To switch between Specification panel tabs

- Click the needed tab in the tab bar. [ATTACHMENT filename='tabs_bar.png']

#### Properties tab

The Properties tab allows you to modify the element properties and manage related element compartments.

##### Working with element properties via the Properties tab of the Specification panel

###### Modifying the declared name of the element via the Specification panel

To modify the declared name of the element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section, p lace the cursor on the Declared Name input field.
3. Edit the declared name as needed.
4. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying the declared short name of the element via the Specification panel

To modify the declared short name of the element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section, p lace the cursor on the Declared Short Name input field.
3. Edit the declared short name as needed.
4. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying the feature type via the Specification panel

To modify the feature type via the Specification panel

1. .
2. Do one of the following:
  1. If the element has no applied types, do one of the following:
    1. Hover over the Type field, then click the Select Type button [ATTACHMENT filename='select type.png'] to display the Type Selection menu. Click the needed type to apply it. The **Type Selection** menu displays both the applied types and those available for selection to replace the already specified type.The **Type Selection**menu has the **Hide Uncommon Elements** filter option enabled by default, which restricts the available selection to compatible types.[IMAGE alt='' src='']The field displays up to 10 elements. When more elements are available, click the label '*+<number of available elements> more*' to expand the field and view all elements. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']The ^ symbol indicates types inherited through redefinition, subsetting, reference subsetting, and feature typing. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
    2. Drag and drop the typing element from the Containment tree on the Type field in the Specification panel.
    3. Select the typing element symbol in a view and use its Drag and Drop handler to drag and drop it on the Type field in the Specification panel. If the type is already set, dragging and dropping the new type replaces the set type with the new one.
  2. If the element already has applied types and you want to add additional ones, do one of the following:
    1. Hover over the Type field, then click the Add Type button [ATTACHMENT filename='select type.png'] to display the Type Selection menu. Click the needed type to apply it.
    2. Select the typing element symbol in a view and use its Drag and Drop handler to drag and drop it on the Type field in the Specification panel while holding the CTRL/Command key.
  3. To remove applied type(s), do one of the following:
    1. Hover over the Type field, then click the Select Type button [ATTACHMENT filename='select type.png'] to display the Type Selection menu. In the menu, click an already applied type to remove it from the element. To remove all applied types, click <UNSPECIFIED> .
    2. In the Type input field of the type you want to remove, click the Unspecify [ATTACHMENT filename='delete_button.png'] button.
3. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying additional properties via the Specification panel

Depending on the element, additional properties may be displayed in the Properties section of the Specification panel. These properties can be specified in the same way as for the .

- Result Expression - displays a r ead-only string of the constraint expression.
- **Asserted Constraint** - displays the asserted constraint's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraints' space=''] page.
- **Assumed Constraint** - displays the assumed constraint's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraints' space=''] page.
- **Required Constraint** - displays the required constraint's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraints' space=''] page.
- **Satisfied Requirement** - displays the satisfied requirement's element name. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Satisfy requirement' space=''] page.
- **Verified Requirement** - displays the verified requirement's element name. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying requirement verification usages for verification case objective' space=''] page.
- Exhibited State - displays the exhibited state's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exhibit state' space='']****page.
- Use Case Included - displays the included use case's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Include use case' space=''] page.
- Performed Action - displays the performed action's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Perform action' space=''] page.
- **Referenced Rendering** - displays the referenced rendering's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Rendering views basics' space=''] page.
- **Event Occurrence** - displays the event occurrence's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Occurrences' space=''] page.
- **Framed Concern** - displays the framed concern's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Frame concern' space=''] page.
- **Trigger**- displays the transition's trigger element name. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space=''] page.
- **Payload**- displays the accept action's payload element name. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Accept action' space=''] page.

###### Modifying the feature value via the Specification panel

To modify the feature value via the Specification panel

1. . In the Value subsection: You can only modify the field for already specified values. Otherwise, it is read-only.To delete the specified value, click the **Delete** [IMAGE alt='' src='']****button next to the field.
  1. To modify the value modifier, click the first dropdown button and click the needed modifier. [ATTACHMENT filename='Modifying_the_feature_value_of_the_element_via_the_Specification_panel.png']
  2. A type-corresponding value editor is displayed:
    1. For integer-typed attributes, click the up or down arrow buttons in the second field to modify the feature value.
    2. For string-typed attributes, input the needed text.
    3. For real-typed attributes, input the needed number.
    4. For boolean-typed attributes, select/deselect the checkbox to specify the needed value.
    5. For enumeration-typed attributes, select the needed value from the dropdown list.
    6. For color-typed attributes, either click the color picker rectangle and pick the color, or input a 6 or 8-digit HEX color code. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Click anywhere outside the edited field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying the documentation of the element via the Specification panel

To modify the documentation of the element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section, p lace the cursor on the Documentation input field.
3. Edit the declared name as needed. To delete the specified documentation, click the **Delete** [IMAGE alt='' src='']****button in the **Documentation**input field. Deleting the field content (using Backspace or Delete keys) does not delete the documentation but rather specifies it as empty.
4. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying the multiplicity of the element via the Specification panel

To modify the multiplicity of the element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section, click the input field under the Multiplicity subsection and do one of the following:
  1. Select the needed multiplicity in the dropdown menu.
  2. Enter the needed multiplicity number.
3. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying the direction of the element via the Specification panel

Direction modification is only available for items, attributes, and refs.

To modify the direction of the element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section, click thefield under the Direction subsection and s elect the needed direction in the dropdown menu.
3. Click anywhere outside the edited field for changes to take effect.

###### Creating an element via the Specification panel

To create an element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears.
3. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu. The selected element is created in the appropriate compartment. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Working with elements via compartments in the Properties tab of the Specification panel

###### Creating an element via the compartment in the Specification panel

To create an element via the compartment in the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the appropriate compartment section's bar, click the **Create <element name>** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button (e.g., *Create attribute*). The element is created in the appropriate compartment. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Deleting an element via the compartment in the Specification panel

To delete an element via the compartment in the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the appropriate compartment's section, hover over the element you want to delete.
3. Click the **Delete [IMAGE alt='' src='']**button. The element is deleted from the appropriate compartment. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Modifying element values via the compartment in the Specification panel

- This procedure is applicable only for string, integer, real, boolean, color, or enumeration-typed attributes and ref elements.
  - If the modification is done for an inherited feature, the inherited feature is redefined with a new value.
  - For modifying other element values, , then see the procedure.
- To delete the specified value, click the Delete [ATTACHMENT filename='delete_button.png'] button next to the field.

To modify element values via the compartment in the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the appropriate compartment's section, click the element whose value you want to modify. A type-corresponding value editor is displayed:
  1. For integer-typed attributes, click the up or down arrow buttons in the second field to modify the feature value.
  2. For string-typed attributes, input the needed text.
  3. For real-typed attributes, input the needed number.
  4. For boolean-typed attributes, select/deselect the checkbox to specify the needed value.
  5. For enumeration-typed attributes, select the needed value from the dropdown list.
  6. For color-typed attributes, either click the color picker rectangle and pick the color, or input a 6 or 8-digit HEX color code. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Click anywhere outside the edited field for changes to take effect.

###### Redefining inherited features via the compartment in the Specification panel

The element features displayed in the compartments are grouped under labels indicating inherited and redefined features.

To redefine inherited features via the compartment in the Specification panel

1. and do one of the following:
  1. To redefine a string, integer, real, boolean, color, or enumeration-typed inherited attributes or ref elements, see the procedure. [ATTACHMENT filename='Modifying element values via the compartment in the Specification panel.png']
  2. To redefine any inherited feature, do one of the following:
    1. H over over the inherited feature you want to redefine and click the **More**button [IMAGE alt='' src=''],****then in the dropdown menu, click **Redefine**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
    2. H over over the inherited feature you want to redefine and click the **Navigate**button **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**to open the Specification panel for the selected feature. At the top of the Specification panel, click the **Redefine** button in the informational message. The properties in the panel are read-only until redefinition is done to prevent modification of non-redefined property values. Once done, you can modify the redefined feature's values via the procedure. [ATTACHMENT filename='Redefining_inherited_elements_via_the_compartment_in_the_Specification_panel.png'] Redefining the inherited metadata feature without a set default valueTo redefine the inherited metadata feature without a set default value, click the **Add** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button in the metadata compartment section's bar. In the submenu, hover over the needed metadata, and in its submenu, click the feature you want to redefine. The feature is redefined and displayed in the metadata compartment under the label of the applied metadata's name. NOTE:The **Add**button is not displayed if all features are already redefined or the element inherits a feature with a set default value.

##### Using the Find in the Specification panel

To use the Find functionality in the Specification panel

1. and press Ctrl+F / Cmd+F. The **Find**input field is displayed at the top of the panel.
2. Enter the needed text in the **Find**field. Use the **Next**/ **Previous**buttons to select the findings. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Relations tab

The Relations tab allows you to access all incoming and outgoing relations of the selected element. The tab organizes relations into categories, such as Interface, Feature Typing, Connection, Exhibit, etc.

##### Accessing relations via the Relations tab

To access relations via the Relations tab

1. Click the Relations tab in the tabs bar.
2. Hover over the needed relation and do one of the following:
  1. Click the **Navigate**button **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**to open the relation's Specification panel.
  2. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='expand_button.png'] button and in its submenu, click any of the available commands:
    1. Select in Containment Tree .
    2. Open in Textual Editor .

#### Navigation tab

The Navigation tab allows you to easily navigate from a selected element to its related elements based on their relations. The tab organizes related elements into categories, such as Subsetted Feature, Redefined Feature, Flow, Connection, Type, etc.

##### Navigating elements via the Navigation tab

To navigate elements via the Navigation tab

1. Click the Navigation tab in the tabs bar.
2. Hover over the needed element and do one of the following:
  1. Click the **Navigate**button **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**to open the element's Specification panel.
  2. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='expand_button.png'] button and in its submenu, click any of the available commands:
    1. Select in Containment Tree .
    2. Open in Textual Editor .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The Specification panel </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">allows you to review and modify properties of specific elements, such as the declared name, declared short name, feature type and value, documentation, and others. You can also review inherited features and modify or create new owned elements in the appropriate compartments<span>, which are equivalent to those displayed in view symbols</span>. <span
````
