<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=296125646 space=SYSML2P version=23 -->
## PAGE 00693: Specifying styles for a rule

- page_id: `296125646`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/296125646/Specifying+styles+for+a+rule
- version_number: 23
- version_date: `2026-05-21T20:23:56.622+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Views > Symbol styles > Custom style sheets > Creating style sheets > Creating rules for a style sheet
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you have [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating style sheets' space=''] and the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating rules for a style sheet' space=''] for it, and have specified the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying a condition for a rule' space=''] for the rules, see the procedures on this page for specifying the styles for the rules.

There are two ways you can specify a style for a rule:

- Local specification allows you to specify the style of a specific rule only. See the procedure on this page.
- External specification allows you to create a custom style definition outside the rule itself, which can then be reused for the style specification of different rules. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating reusable styles' space=''] page.

##### Specifying styles for a style sheet rule

To specify styles for a style sheet rule via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating style sheets' space=''] , a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating rules for a style sheet' space=''] for it, and the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying a condition for a rule' space=''] for the rule.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] for the style sheet's rule part usage element.
3. In the Specification panel's style attributes compartment:
  1. Specify the style definition in the Type field:
    1. Click the input field to view the dropdown menu with available style definitions. Click the needed one(s) to specify it as the type. The available style attributes (in the next step) depend on the style definitions you specify in the **Type**field. While you can specify multiple style definitions, keep in mind:Some definitions subclassify others; e.g., the *Shape Style* definition subclassifies *Symbol Style*, making it redundant to use both, as *Shape Style*inherits all attributes of the *Symbol Style*definition.Some definitions apply to all or most symbols (e.g., *Shape Style*, *Symbol Style*, *Compartment Style,*etc*.*), while others affect only specific ones. For example, *Shape On Border Style*can only affect elements that can be displayed as symbols on another symbol's border, such as port, attribute, ref, or item usages.For a list of all style definitions, their attributes, value types, and symbols to which they apply, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Style sheet styles tables' space='']. [ATTACHMENT filename='style definitions dropdown menu.png']
    2. To remove the specified style definition:
      1. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='delete specified style definition button.png'] button next to the style definition. [ATTACHMENT filename='delete specified style definition.png']
      2. To remove all specified style definitions, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='More button for style definition.png'] button in the Type input field, then click Clear All . [ATTACHMENT filename='clear all types.png']
  2. Specify the style attributes by clicking the element whose value you want to modify. A type-corresponding value editor is displayed:
    1. For integer-typed attributes, click the up or down arrow buttons in the second field to modify the feature value.
    2. For string-typed attributes, input the needed text.
    3. For real-typed attributes, input the needed number.
    4. For boolean or enumeration-typed attributes, select the needed value from the dropdown list.
    5. For color-typed attributes, either click the color picker rectangle and pick the color, or input a 6 or 8-digit HEX color code. If the modification is done for an inherited feature listed in the **Inherited**section of the compartment, the inherited feature is redefined with a new value and displayed in the **Owned**section.To delete the specified value, hover over it and click the **Delete** [IMAGE alt='' src='']****button. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  3. Click anywhere outside the edited field for changes to take effect.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Assessing layout-sensitive changes' space=''] .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Once you have <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating style sheets" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[created a style sheet]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating rules for a style sheet" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[rules]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for it, and have specified the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specifying a condition for a rule" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[conditions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for the rules, see the procedures on this page for specifying the styles for the rules.</p><p>There are two ways you can specify a style for a rule:</p><ul><li>Local specification allows you to specify the style of a specific rule only. See the procedure <ac:link ac:anchor="Specifying styles for a style sheet rule" /> on this page. </li><li>External specification allows you to create a custom style definition outside the rule itself, which can then be reused for the style specification of different rules. See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating reusable styles" /></ac:link> page.</li></ul><h3>Specifying styles for a style sheet rule</h3><p>To specify styles for a style sheet rule via the Specification panel</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="dc7a7530-cb6a-4a9e-9b02-a0360930f61b"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating style sheets" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create a style sheet]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating rules for a style sheet" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[rule]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for it, and the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specifying a condition for a rule" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[condition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for the rule.</li><li data-uuid="09bd8c38-a6a9-4c9a-a803-caa891c31d20"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for the style sheet's <em>rule</em> part usage element.</li><li data-uuid="9336d564-3d9f-440b-89ab-a7f35a668339">In the Specification panel's <strong>style attributes </strong>compartment:<ol><li data-uuid="674fd42d-5cbe-4a81-b399-3eff66cd9792">Specify the style definition in the <strong>Type </strong>field:<ol><li data-uuid="39dc42a2-eaa6-456f-89d9-6218b06f4a87">Click the input field to view the dropdown menu with available style definitions. Click the needed one(s) to specify it as the type.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bfc828fe-3c98-455c-9126-278b50717435"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The available style attributes (in the next step) depend on the style definitions you specify in the <strong>Type </strong>field. While you can specify multiple style definitions, keep in mind:</p><ul><li data-uuid="30eef3a3-53ff-4a82-af7d-5f4a6b01f2ed">Some definitions subclassify others; e.g., the <em>Shape Style</em> definition subclassifies <em>Symbol Style</em>, making it redundant to use both, as <em>Shape Style </em>inherits all attributes of the <em>Symbol Style </em>definition.</li><li data-uuid="31b75b64-8449-4144-b3fd-0ad334a7f338">Some definitions apply to all or most symbols (e.g., <em>Shape Style</em>, <em>Symbol Style</em>, <em>Compartment Style, </em>etc<em>.</em>), while others affect only specific ones. For example, <em>Shape On Border Style </em>can only affect elements that can be displayed as symbols on another symbol's border, such as port, attribute, ref, or item usages.</li></ul><p>For a list of all style definitions, their attributes, value types, and symbols to which they apply, see the <ac:link ac:anchor="Symbol styles table"><ri:page ri:content-title="Style sheet styles tables" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Symbol styles table]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="style definitions dropdown menu.png" /></ac:image></li><li data-uuid="aeb8027f-4f3d-418b-b508-433512c37d5a">To remove the specified style definition:<ol><li data-uuid="edb4e49b-4618-4cc7-b771-b752cde9efe1">Click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="delete specified style definition button.png" /></ac:image> button next to the style definition.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="delete specified style definition.png" /></ac:image></li><li data-uuid="1f904e30-5774-4650-b4a2-22ce024dfc5e">To remove all specified style definitions, click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="More button for style definition.png" /></ac:image>button in the <strong>Type</strong> input field, then click <strong>Clear All</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="clear all types.png" /></ac:image></li></ol></li></ol></li><li data-uuid="ec5940b9-512e-4227-bff1-1400ac1ce77a">Specify the style attributes by clicking the element whose value you want to modify. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">A type-corresponding value editor is displayed:</span><ol><li>For integer-typed attributes, click the up or down arrow buttons in the second field to modify the feature value. </li><li>For string-typed attributes, input the needed text.</li><li>For real-typed attributes, input the needed number.</li><li>For boolean or enumeration-typed attributes, select the needed value from the dropdown list.</li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">For color-typed attributes, either click the color picker rectangle and pick the color, or input a 6 or 8-digit HEX color code.<br /></span><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e0b45e7-78f9-4f22-9ab2-53f9cfc275db"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li data-uuid="7d9e4c01-ea6e-42e6-ad28-dd314c6a8a53">If the modification is done for an inherited feature listed in the <strong>Inherited </strong>section of the compartment, the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">inherited feature is redefined with a new value and displayed in the <strong>Owned </strong>section.</span></li><li data-uuid="4c022ca5-9818-432c-a9f8-0e43f2d532cf">To delete the specified value, hover over it and click the <strong>Delete</strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="delete_button.png" /></ac:image><strong> </strong>button.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="type-corresponding value editor.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li></ol></li><li data-uuid="36b4b247-8e4a-461a-9393-2abb7e1f896c">Click anywhere outside the edited field for changes to take effect.</li></ol></li><li data-uuid="70ae9f83-5753-40eb-94d4-f35659cea005"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Assessing layout-sensitive changes" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Assess layout-sensitive changes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303464459 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00694: Start action

- page_id: `303464459`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/303464459/Start+action
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-24T14:24:25.040+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > States
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A start action represents the start of a sequence.

##### Creating a start action

###### Creating a start action via the Textual Editor

To create a start action via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and create a transition. Specify the keyword start as the source state of the transition. Although *start*appears as an element name in the Textual Editor, it is a keyword for the **start**action. It cannot be named or renamed.
2. Click the Synchronize button. stateinTransport{firststartthenload;...}

###### Creating a start action via the view palette

To create a start actionvia the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the States group, click the start button for a start action.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the element symbol.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A start action represents the start of a sequence.</p><h3>Creating a start action</h3><h4>Creating a start action via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To create a start action via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and create a transition. Specify the keyword <em>start </em>as the source state of the transition.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3e54883e-0663-4bf2-83f9-6513529d0d6c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Although <em>start </em>appears as an element name in the Textual Editor, it is a keyword for the <strong>start </strong>action. It cannot be named or renamed.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae630f5a-39fc-43ac-aa7b-b45e25fd9b91"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">state</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">inTransport </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">{</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">    first</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">start</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">then</span><span style="color:var(--ds-chart-gray-bold,#8590a2);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">load</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">...</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Creating a start action via the view palette</h4><p>To create a start action<span> via the view palette</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the States group, click the <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">start</strong> button for a start action.</li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the element symbol.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577546 space=SYSML2P version=5 -->
## PAGE 00695: Start and done actions

- page_id: `249577546`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577546/Start+and+done+actions
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-09-22T13:44:07.949+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions > Controlling actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A start action and a done action represent the start and end of an action sequence.

##### Creating start and done actions

###### Creating start and done actions via the Textual Editor

To create start and done actions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *start*for a start action.
  2. done for a done action. Although *start*and *done*appear as element names in the Textual Editor, they are keywords for start and done actions. However, they cannot be named or renamed.
2. Click the Synchronize button. actiontransportPassenger{ 
firststart; 
...thendone;}

###### Creating control nodes via the view palette

To create control nodes via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Actions group, click one of the following:
  1. **start** or a start action.
  2. done for a done action.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the action symbol.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A start action and a done action represent the start and end of an action sequence.</p><h3>Creating start and done actions</h3><h4>Creating start and done actions via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To create start and done actions via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:<ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><em>start </em>for a start action.</p></li><li><em>done </em>for a done action.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3e54883e-0663-4bf2-83f9-6513529d0d6c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Although <em>start </em>and <em>done </em>appear as element names in the Textual Editor, they are keywords for start and done actions. However, they cannot be named or renamed.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae630f5a-39fc-43ac-aa7b-b45e25fd9b91"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">action</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">transportPassenger</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">first</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">start</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">...<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">then</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">done</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Creating control nodes via the view palette</h4><p>To create <span>control nodes via the view palette</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Actions group, click one of the following:<ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">start</strong> or a start action.</p></li><li><strong>done </strong>for a done action.</li></ol></li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the action symbol.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577645 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00696: State decomposition

- page_id: `249577645`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577645/State+decomposition
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-04-20T12:59:35.335+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > States
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

State definitions and state usages can be decomposed into lower-level states to create a hierarchy of states. You can define a state usage by a state definition, and a state usage can be subsetted or redefined. This provides enhanced flexibility to modify a hierarchy of state usages to adapt to its context.

##### Decomposing state definitions

You can represent the next level of decomposition of a state definition using the feature typing (*defined by*) relationship.

###### Decomposing state definitions via the Textual Editor

To decompose state definitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, identify the state usage that you want to define, nested within a state definition.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Feature typing' space=''] .

statedefonOffStates{ 
stateon:OnState; // state usage, owned by a state definition, defined by another state definition 
stateoff:OffState; 
} 
statedefOnState; 
statedefOffState;

###### Displaying decompositions of state definitions in a view

To display decompositions of state definitions in a view

- In a view, select the state definition symbol and the state usage symbol it defines, then use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command. The feature typing relationship is displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Decomposing state usages

You can display a state tree representing a composition of a state from other states.

###### Decomposing state usages via the Textual Editor

To decompose state usages via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the state you want to decompose, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating states' space=''] .
2. Click the Synchronize button.

statedeviceStates:onOffStates{ // the owning state usage with nested state usages 
stateon:OnState; 
stateoff:OffState; 
}

###### Decomposing state usages via the view palette by creating a composite state

To decompose state usages via the view palette by creating a composite state

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating states' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the state for which you want to create a composite state.
2. Create a composite state in one of the following ways:
  1. Via the view palette (state symbol option):
    1. In the view palette, under the States group, click the state button to create a state usage element.
    2. Click on the state symbol displayed in the view pane to create a state owned by another.
  2. Via the view palette (feature membership option):
    1. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
    2. First, click on the state symbol displayed in the view pane and then click anywhere in the view pane to create a state owned by another.

###### Decomposing state usages via the view palette by designating an existing state as a composite one

To decompose state usages via the view palette by designating an existing state as a composite one

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating states' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the states you want to designate as the owner and the owned.
2. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
3. First, click on the state symbol you want to designate as the owner, then the one you want to designate as the owned one.

###### Displaying decompositions of state usages in a view

To display decompositions of state usages in a view

- In a view, select the owning state usage symbol and use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command under the Tree-View Style category. The feature membership and the owned features (state usages) are displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Reusing and modifying the state tree

A state can subset another state, facilitating state tree reuse and modification. The subsetting state inherits the state decomposition and other features. You can then modify the inherited states and features through redefinition and subsetting in addition to adding new states.

###### Reusing and modifying the states tree via the Textual Editor

To reuse and modify the states tree via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating states' space=''] you want to reuse, then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Subsetting' space=''] it.
2. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Redefinition' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Subsetting' space=''] the state features in the element body.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

statedeviceStates:onOffStates{// subsetted state 
stateon:OnState; 
stateoff:OffState; 
}

state heaterStates :> deviceStates {// a state reuses the states tree by subsetting another state 
 state :>> on; 
 state :>> off; 
}

###### Reusing the states tree via the view palette

To reuse the states tree via the view palette

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating states' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the state whose decomposition you want to reuse.
2. In the view palette, under the Specializations group, click the subsetting button.
3. Click on the state you want to denote as the subsetting one and then click on the state you want to denote as the subsetted (reused) one.

###### Displaying the state tree reuse in a view

To display the state tree reuse in a view

1. In a view, select the subsetting and the subsetted state usage symbols, then u se the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command . The subsetting relationship is displayed.
2. (Optional) To display owned features of the subsetting or subsetted states, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command. The owned features (state usages) are displayed. [ATTACHMENT filename='Displaying the state tree reuse on a diagram.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>State definitions and state usages can be decomposed into lower-level states to create a hierarchy of states. You can define a state usage by a state definition, and a state usage can be subsetted or redefined. This provides enhanced flexibility to modify a hierarchy of state usages to adapt to its context.</p><h3>Decomposing state definitions</h3><p>You can represent the next level of decomposition of a state definition using the feature typing (<em>defined by</em>) relationship.</p><h4>Decomposing state definitions via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To decompose state definitions via the Textual Editor"><ri:page ri:content-title="Feature typing" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Define the state by the state definition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </li></ol><ac:structured-macro style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">OnState</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;     <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// state usage, owned by a state definition, defined by another state definition </span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">OnState</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">state</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">OffState</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Displaying decompositions of state definitions in a view</h4><p>To display decompositions of state definitions in a view</p><hr /><ul><li>In a view, select the state definition symbol and the state usage symbol it defines, then use the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display In-Between Relations]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>command. <br />The feature typing relationship is displayed.</li></ul><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Displaying decompositions of state definitions on a diagram.png" /></ac:image></p><h3>Decomposing state usages</h3><p>You can display a state tree representing a composition of a state from other states.</p><h4>Decomposing state you want to decompose, </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating states" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create the new state usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="698a77ad-c59e-4286-9cdd-a7902f3f2cf1"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">state</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">deviceStates </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">: </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">onOffStates </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">{ <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">   <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// the owning state usage with nested state usages</span></span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">state</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">on</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">OnState</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">state</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">off</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">OffState</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Decomposing state usages via the view palette by creating a composite state</h4><p>To decompose state usages via the view palette by creating a composite state</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating states" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[display]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the state for which you want to create a composite state.</li><li>Create a composite state in one of the following ways:<ol><li>Via the view palette (state symbol option):<ol><li>In the view palette, under the States group, click the <strong>state</strong><span> button to create a state usage element.</span></li><li>Click on the state symbol displayed in the view pane to create a state owned by another. </li></ol></li><li>Via the view palette (feature membership option):<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the <strong>feature membership</strong> <span>button.</span></li><li>First, click on the state symbol displayed in the view pane and then click anywhere in the view pane to create a state owned by another.</li></ol></li></ol></li></ol><h4>Decomposing state usages via the view palette by designating an existing state as a composite one</h4><p>To decompose state usages via the view palette by designating an existing state as a composite one</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating states" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[display]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the states you want to designate as the owner and the owned.</li><li>In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the <strong>feature membership</strong> <span>button.</span></li><li>First, click on the state symbol you want to designate as the owner, then the one you want to designate as the owned one.</li></ol><h4>Displaying decompositions of state usages in a view</h4><p>To display decompositions of state usages in a view</p><hr /><ul><li>In a view, select the owning state usage symbol and use the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display Features]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>command under the <strong>Tree-View Style</strong> category. <br />The feature membership and the owned features (state usages) are displayed. </li></ul><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Displaying decompositions of state usages on a diagram.png" /></ac:image></p><h3>Reusing and modifying the state tree</h3><p>A state can subset another state, facilitating state tree reuse and modification. The subsetting state inherits the state decomposition and other features. You can then modify the inherited states and features through redefinition and subsetting in addition to adding new states.</p><h4>Reusing and modifying the states tree via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To reuse and modify the states tree via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating states" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[declare the state usage]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> you want to reuse, then <ac:link ac:anchor="Subsetting a usage via the Textual Editor"><ri:page ri:content-title="Subsetting" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[subset]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> it.</li><li>(Optional) <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Redefinition" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Redefine]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Subsetting" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[subset]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the state features in the element body.</li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8811e49e-ed80-4671-a9c5-db96a9276d25"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">: </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">onOffStates </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">            <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// subsetted state</span></span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">state</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">deviceStates</span> {<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">        <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// a state reuses the states tree by subsetting another state</span></span><br />    <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">state</span> :&gt;&gt; <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">on</span>;<br />    <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">state</span> :&gt;&gt; <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">off</span>;<br />}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Reusing the states tree via the view palette</h4><p>To reuse the states tree via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating states" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[display]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the state whose decomposition you want to reuse. </li><li>In the view palette, under the Specializations group, click the <strong>subsetting </strong>button.</li><li>Click on the state you want to denote as the subsetting one and then click on the state you want to denote as the subsetted (reused) one.</li></ol><h4>Displaying the state tree reuse in a view</h4><p>To display the state tree reuse in a view</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, select the subsetting and the subsetted state usage symbols, then u<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">se the </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display In-Between Relations]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">command</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">. The subsetting relationship is displayed.</span></li><li>(Optional) To display owned features of the subsetting or subsetted states, use the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display Features]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>command. The owned features (state usages) are displayed.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Displaying the state tree reuse on a diagram.png" /></ac:image></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576748 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00698: Status Line

- page_id: `249576748`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576748/Status+Line
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-02-27T19:40:41.115+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Project management > Modeling tool environment > User interface
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Status line is located at the bottom of the modeling tool window. It displays various indications of active notifications, errors, the progress of currently running tasks, and some useful commands.

| Status bar icon | Status bar icon name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Zoom Out | Click to zoom out on the current view. |
|  | Zoom In | Click to zoom in on the current view. |
|  | View Zoom Slider | Click the zoom slider's cursor to zoom in or out of the current view. |
|  | Fit To Window | Click to fit the current view in the window. |
|  | Zoom 1:1 | Click to restore the view to its original size. |
|  | Enter Presentation Mode | Click to enter the presentation mode. |
|  | Open Notification Window | Click to open the notification window. |
|  | Background Tasks | Click to view the background tasks. |
|  | Memory Monitor | Displays the memory used by the modeling tool. |

##### Managing the display of the Status line's commands

To manage the display of some of the Status line's commands

- In the main menu, click View > Status line .
- In the main menu, click Options > Environment . In the open dialog, select General > Display .

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577549 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00700: Structured control actions

- page_id: `249577549`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577549/Structured+control+actions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-01-28T09:03:44.526+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions > Controlling actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Structured control action usages are used to control the performance of nested action usages in a structured way. There are three kinds of structured control action usages:

- An if action usage evaluates a condition expression and then performs a then clause action usage if the expression evaluates to true , or, optionally, an else clause action usage if the expression evaluates to false .
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='If action' space=''] page.
- A while loop action usage performs a body clause action usage iteratively, as long as its while expression continues to evaluate to true and its until expression continues to evaluate to false .
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='While and for loop actions' space=''] page.
- A for loop action usage performs a body clause action usage iteratively, assigning a loop variable successively for each iteration to the values resulting from the evaluation of a sequence expression.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='While and for loop actions' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Structured control action usages are used to control the performance of nested action usages in a structured way. There are three kinds of structured control action usages:</p><ul><li>An <strong>if</strong> <strong>action usage</strong> evaluates a condition expression and then performs a then clause action usage if the expression evaluates to <em>true</em>, or, optionally, an else clause action usage if the expression evaluates to <em>false</em>.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="If action" /></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li><li>A <strong>while loop action usage </strong>performs a<strong> </strong>body clause action usage iteratively, as long as its while expression continues to evaluate to <em>true</em> and its until expression continues to evaluate to <em>false</em>.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="While and for loop actions" /></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li><li>A <strong>for loop action usage </strong>performs a<strong> </strong>body clause action usage iteratively, assigning a loop variable successively for each iteration to the values resulting from the evaluation of a sequence expression.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="While and for loop actions" /></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li></ul>
````

- . Contains style definitions, their attributes and value types, including primary style definitions and predefined style definitions.
- . Contains style definitions, their attributes and value types, including primary compartment style definitions and predefined compartment style definitions.

| Feature Styleview def FeatureStyle :> BasicFeatureStyle { attribute showInheritedSign : Boolean;}+ also has attributes inherited from BasicFeatureStyle | - | Majority of the element metaclassesViewUsage ViewDefinition PartDefinition PartUsage PortDefinition PortUsage UseCaseUsageUseCaseDefinition LiteralExpression CalculationUsageCalculationDefinition ExhibitStateUsage StateUsageStateDefinition PerformActionUsageActionUsageActionDefinitionEnumerationDefinitionEnumerationUsage AttributeDefinition AttributeUsage ReferenceUsage ConcernUsageConcernDefinition RequirementUsageRequirementDefinition ItemDefinition ItemUsage ConstraintUsageConstraintDefinitionOperatorExpression EventOccurrenceUsageOccurrenceUsageOccurrenceDefinition Classifier NamespaceExcludes:Package Documentation CommentSymbol kinds:ForkJoinDecideMergeSwimlane |
| Connector Styleview def ConnectorStyle :> BasicFeatureStyle { attribute showPayload : Boolean;}+ also has attributes inherited from BasicFeatureStyle | - | Connectors displayed as paths |
| Rectangular Shape Styleview def RectangularShapeStyle :> SeparatorStyle, TextBoxStyle { attribute isRounded : Boolean;}+ also has attributes inherited from SeparatorStyle, TextBoxStyle | - | Rectangular shape**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Text Box Styleview def TextBoxStyle :> ShapeStyle;+ has attributes inherited from ShapeStyle | - | Text box shape**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Linear Separator Styleview def LinearSeparatorStyle :> SeparatorStyle { attribute orientation : OrientationEnum;}+ also has attributes inherited from SeparatorStyle | - | Separator shape**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Separator Styleview def SeparatorStyle :> SymbolStyle { attribute textHorizontalPosition : TextHorizontalPositionEnum; attribute lineStyle : LineStyleEnum;}+ also has attributes inherited from SymbolStyle | - | Separator shape**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Bar Shape Styleview def BarShapeStyle :> ShapeStyle { attribute orientation : OrientationEnum;}+ also has attributes inherited from ShapeStyle | - | Fork*Join**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Swimlanes Styleview def SwimlanesStyle :> ShapeStyle { attribute lineStyle : LineStyleEnum;}+ also has attributes inherited from ShapeStyleLocation: DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::SysMLStyles | - | Swimlane**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Path Styleview def PathStyle :> SymbolStyle { attribute pathStyle : PathStyleEnum; attribute labelOrientation : LabelOrientationEnum;}+ also has attributes inherited from SymbolStyle | - | Path**symbol kind, not metaclas |
| Path End Shape Styleview def PathEndShapeStyle :> SymbolStyle { attribute labelPosition : LabelPositionEnum; attribute labelOrientation : LabelOrientationEnum;}+ also has attributes inherited from SymbolStyle | - | Path end shape labels**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Image Shape Styleview def ImageShapeStyle :> ShapeStyle;+ has attributes inherited from ShapeStyle | - | Image shape**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Comment Styleview def CommentStyle :> ElementStyle { attribute showLocale : Boolean;}+ also has attributes inherited from ElementStyle | - | CommentDocumentation |
| Note Styleview def NoteStyle :> ShapeStyle { #InvisibleInDialog attribute :>> contentFillColor; #InvisibleInDialog attribute :>> textVerticalPosition; #InvisibleInDialog attribute :>> compartmentFillColor; #InvisibleInDialog attribute :>> compartmentTextColor;}+ also has attributes inherited from ShapeStyle | - | Note**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Anchor Styleview def AnchorStyle :> PathStyle;+ has attributes inherited from PathStyle | - | Anchor**symbol kind, not metaclass |
| Package Shape Styleview def PackageShapeStyle :> ShapeStyle { attribute showNameInTab : Boolean;}+ has attributes inherited from ShapeStyle | - | Package |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577339 space=SYSML2P version=7 -->
## PAGE 00702: Subclassification

- page_id: `249577339`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577339/Subclassification
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-10-08T13:55:21.830+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Relations > Specializations
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Use the subclassification relationshipto specialize a definition element. The specialized definition inherits the features of the more general definition element and can add other features.

##### Subclassification

###### Subclassifying a definition via the Textual Editor

To subclassify a definition via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, identify the definition element that you want to be the specializing one.
2. Follow the element name with either one of the following:
  1. keyword specializes
  2. textual symbol :>
3. Follow the keyword with the name of the specialized definition element.
4. Click the **Synchronize**button. partdefVehicle;partdefDieselVehicle:>Vehicle;/* or */partdefDieselVehiclespecializesVehicle;

###### Subclassifying a definition via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To subclassify a definition via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on an element symbol executing the Set Superclassifier command.

###### Subclassifying a definition via the view palette

To subclassify a definition via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Specializations group, select subclassification .
2. Draw a line with the arrowhead going from the specific definition element to the general one. [ATTACHMENT filename='Subclassification.png']

###### Subclassifying a definitionvia the Create Relation command

To subclassify a definition via the Create Relation command

1. Right-click the element that you want to be the specializing one.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relation , then in the submenu, under Outgoing , click subclassification . The Create... dialog opens.
3. In the Create... dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to specialize. Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to specialize and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to specialize from the Containment tree onto the Create... dialog.
4. In the Create... dialog, click OK .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577881 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00703: Subjects, actors, and objectives for cases

- page_id: `249577881`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577881/Subjects+actors+and+objectives+for+cases
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-19T15:50:09.977+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Analysis & Verification > Case
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you create the needed case for your model, you can specify it further. Cases are usually specified with:

- The subject of a case is modeled as a distinguished parameter, similarly to the subject of a requirement.
- The objective** of a case is modeled as a requirement usage to be satisfied by the performance of the case.
- A case definition or usage may also have one or more actor parameters that represent roles played by an entity external to the subject of the case but necessary to the specification of the case.

A case can have only one subject and objective.

##### Specifying subject, actors, and objective for case

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Textual Editor

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the curly braces of the case's body, do the following:
  1. To specify a subject, declare the subject keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  2. To specify an actor, declare the actor keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  3. To specify an objective, declare the objective keyword, then in the element body, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Annotations' space=''] .
2. Click the Synchronize button. casedefFaultRecovery{// case definition 
subjectsystem:AutomationSystem; // case subject 
actorengineer:Person; // case actor 
objective{ // case objective 
doc 
/* The engineer determines the cause of the systemfault and resolves it returning the system tonominal operation.*/ 
} 
}

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's Drag and Drop handler on a case element symbol executing the **Create Subject,****Create Actor** ,**Create Objective**, or**Create Require Constraint of Objective** command.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] page.

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the symbol compartment in a view

- Do one of the following:
  - Select a case symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create. The new element is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.
  - Select a case symbol and on the right side of the actors compartment, click the Create actor button. The new element is created and displayed in the actors compartment.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Specification panel

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **actors**compartment section's bar, click the **Create actor**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element is created in the appropriate compartment.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The new element is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.</li><li>Select a case symbol and on the right side of the <strong>actors </strong>compartment, click the<span> </span><strong>Create <span>actor </span></strong>button. The new element is created and displayed in the <strong>actors </strong>compartment.</li></ul></li></ul><h4>Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Specification panel</h4><p>To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Specification panel</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Do one of the following:</span><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the Properties section bar, click the <strong>Create Element</strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> button. The shortcut menu appears. </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the <strong>actors </strong>compartment section's bar, click the <strong>Create actor</strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_compartment_via_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> button. <br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The element is created in the appropriate compartment.</span></li></ol></li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577483 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00704: Subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases

- page_id: `249577483`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577483/Subjects+actors+and+objectives+for+use+cases
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-19T15:38:15.951+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Use cases
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A use case is typically specified as a sequence of interactions between the subject and the various actors, which are all modeled as part usages. The objective of the use case is to provide an observable result of value to one or more of its actors.

A use case can have only one subject and objective.

##### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via Textual Editor

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the curly braces of the use case's body, do the following:
  1. To specify a subject, declare the subject keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  2. To specify an actor, declare the actor keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  3. To specify an objective, declare the objective keyword, then in the element body , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Annotations' space=''] .
2. Click the Synchronize button. usecasedef'Provide Transportation'{ 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; // use case subject 
actordriver:Person; // use case actor 
actorenvironment:Environment;objective{ // use case objective 
doc/* Transport driver and passengers from starting location to ending location. */ 
}}

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on a use case element symbol, executing the **Create Subject,** **Create Actor,** **Create Objective**, or**Create Require Constraint of Objective** command.
- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on the specific subject or actor displayed on the use case element symbol, executing the **Set Subsetted Feature** **** command.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] page.

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the symbol compartment in a view

- Do one of the following:
  - Select a use case symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create. The new element is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.
  - Select a use case symbol and on the right side of the actors compartment, click the Create actor button. The new element is created and displayed in the actors compartment.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the Specification panel

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **actors**compartment section's bar, click the **Create actor**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element is created in the appropriate compartment.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577375 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00705: Subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements

- page_id: `249577375`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577375/Subjects+actors+and+stakeholders+for+requirements
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-19T15:31:06.598+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Requirements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you create the needed requirements for your model, you can specify them further. Requirements are usually specified with subject, actor, and stakeholder parameters.

- A subject is a distinguished parameter that identifies the entity on which the requirement is being specified.
- An **actor**parameter is a part usage representing an entity that plays a special role relative to therequirement definition or usage. A requirement may have multiple actors.
- A **stakeholder**parameter is a part usage representing a role played by an entity (usually a person, organization, or other group) having concerns related to the containing requirement. A requirement may have multiple stakeholders.

##### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Textual Editor

To specify subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the curly braces of the requirement's body, do any of the following:
  1. To specify the subject, declare the subject keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional)
  2. To specify the actor, declare the actor keyword, specify the element name, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  3. To specify the stakeholder, declare the stakeholder keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional).
2. Click the Synchronize button. requirementvehicleSpecification{ 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; 
 actor environment : Environment; 
 stakeholder client : Clients;}

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To specify subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's Drag and Drop handler on a requirement element symbol executing the **Create Actor**,**Create Subject**, or**Create Stakeholder** command.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] page.

To specify subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the symbol compartment in a view

- Do one of the following:
  - Select a requirement symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create.
  - Select a requirement symbol and on the right side of the needed compartment, click the Create <element> button. The element is created and displayed in the appropriate compartment.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Specification panel

To specify subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the needed element compartment's section bar, click the **Create <element>** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element is created in the appropriate compartment.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE


<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577885 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00706: Subjects, objectives, and return parameters for analysis cases

- page_id: `249577885`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577885/Subjects+objectives+and+return+parameters+for+analysis+cases
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-10-10T14:21:39.540+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Analysis & Verification > Analysis case
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you create the needed analysis case for your model, you can specify it further.An analysis case is comprised of a set of steps with an objective to evaluate a result about a subject of the analysis. Each step can be an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating actions' space=''], a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Calculation' space=''], or an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Analysis case' space=''].

Analysis cases are typically specified with:

- The subject of an analysis case is modeled as a distinguished parameter, similar to the subject of a requirement, identifying what is being analyzed.
- The objective is a requirement on the result of the analysis case, allowing both [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Assume constraint' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Require constraint' space=''] constraints.
- The return parameter for the analysis result, as for a calculation.

A case can have only one subject, objective, and return parameter.

##### Specifying subject, objective, and return parametersfor analysis case

###### Specifying subjects, objectives, and return parameters for analysis cases via the Textual Editor

To specify subjects, objectives, and return parameters for analysis cases via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the curly braces of the case's body, do the following:
  1. To specify a subject, declare the subject keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  2. To specify an objective, declare the objective keyword, then in the element body, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Annotations' space=''] .
  3. To specify an analysis result, declare the return keyword followed by the result parameter.
2. Click the Synchronize button. analysisdefFuelEconomyAnalysis{ 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; 
returnfuelEconomyResult:DistancePerVolumeValue; 
objectivefuelEconomyAnalysisObjective{ 
doc 
/*The objective of this analysis is to determine whether thesubject vehicle can satisfy the fuel economy requirement.*/ 
requirement:FuelEconomyRequirement; 
} 
// ... 
}

###### Specifying subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To specify subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's Drag and Drop handler on an analysis case element symbol executing the **Create Subject, Create Objective**, or **Create Require Constraint of Objective** command.

###### Specifying subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] page.

To specify subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the symbol compartment in a view

- Select an analysis case symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create. The new element is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.

###### Specifying subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the Specification panel

To specify subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''].
2. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu. The element is created in the appropriate compartment.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE


<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577889 space=SYSML2P version=5 -->
## PAGE 00707: Subjects, objectives, and verdicts for verification cases

- page_id: `249577889`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577889/Subjects+objectives+and+verdicts+for+verification+cases
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-09-22T13:12:30.796+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Analysis & Verification > Verification case
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A verification case is a kind of case whose result is a verdict on whether the subject of the case satisfies certain requirements.

- The subject of a verification case is an input parameter that identifies the system or other entity that is being evaluated as to whether it satisfies certain requirements.
- The objective of a verification case is to verify that the verification subject satisfies one or more specific requirements, which are specified as a special kind of required constraint in the objective.
  - In addition to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Requirement constraints' space=''] usages allowed in any requirement usage, the objective of a verification case may also have **requirement verification usages**, which indicate the requirements to be verified by the verification case. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying requirement verification usages for verification case objective' space=''] page.
- The return parameter called a verdict, which can be:
  - Pass indicates that the subject has been determined to satisfy the requirements to be verified.
  - Fail indicates that the subject has been determined not to satisfy the requirements to be verified.
  - Inconclusive indicates that a determination could not be made as to whether the subject satisfies the requirements to be verified.
  - Error indicates that an error occurred during the performance of the verification.

- A verification case can have only one subject, objective, and return parameter.
- A verification case always returns a verdict of type VerdictKind (in the library package VerificationCases ), which is an enumeration with allowed values pass , fail , inconclusive , and error .

##### Specifying subject, objective, and verdictfor verification case

###### Specifying subjects, objectives, and verdicts for verification cases via the Textual Editor

To specify subjects, objectives, and verdicts for verification cases via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the curly braces of the case's body, do the following:
  1. To specify a subject, declare the subject keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  2. To specify an objective, declare the objective keyword, then in the element body, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Annotations' space=''] .
  3. To specify a verdict, declare the return keyword named verdict, defined by VerdictKind .
2. Click the Synchronize button. verificationdefVehicleMassTest{ 
subjecttestVehicle:Vehicle; 
objectivevehicleMassVerificationObjective{ 
verifyvehicleMassRequirement; 
} 
returnverdict:VerificationCases::VerdictKind; 
}

###### Specifying subjects and objectives for verification cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To specify subjects and objectives for verification cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's Drag and Drop handler on a verification case element symbol executing the **Create Subject**,**Create Objective**, or **Create Require Constraint of Objective** command.

###### Specifying subjects and objectives for verification cases via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] page.

To specify subjects and objectives for verification cases via the symbol compartment in a view

- Select a verification case symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create. The new element is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.

###### Specifying subjects and objectives for verification cases via the Specification panel

To specify subjects and objectives for verification cases via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''].
2. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu. The element is created in the appropriate compartment.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
