<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577345 space=SYSML2P version=5 -->
## PAGE 00708: Subsetting

- page_id: `249577345`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577345/Subsetting
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2026-02-17T20:59:53.417+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Relations > Specializations
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Use the subsetting relationshipto specialize a usage element. Inherited features can be subsetted by multiple features. The specialized definition inherits the features of the more general definition element and can add other features.

##### Subsetting

###### Subsetting a usage via the Textual Editor

To subset a usage via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, identify the usage element that you want to be the subsetting one.
2. Follow the element name with either one of the following:
  1. keyword subsets
  2. textual symbol :>
3. Follow the keyword with the name of the subsetted usage element.
4. Click the Synchronize button. partengine{ 
partvalves; 
} 
partdieselEngine:>engine;/* or */partdieselEnginesubsetsengine;

###### Subsetting a usage via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To subset a usage via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on an element symbol executing the Set Subsetted Feature command.

To create a new element with the subsetting relationship between the source and target, use the **Create Subsetting Usage**command.

###### Subsetting a usage via the view palette

To subset a usage via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Specializations group, select subsetting .
2. Draw the line with the arrowhead going from the subsetting usage to the subsetted one. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Subsetting a usage via the Create Relation command

To subset a usagevia the Create Relation command

1. Right-click the element that you want to be the subsetting one.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relation , then in the submenu, under Outgoing , click subsetting . The Create... dialog opens.
3. In the Create New... dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to subset. Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to subset and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to subset from the Containment tree onto the Create... dialog.
4. In the Create... dialog, click OK .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Use the subsetting relationship </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">to specialize a usage element. Inherited features can be subsetted by multiple features. The specialized definition inherits the features of the more general definition element and can add other features.</span></p><h3>Subsetting</h3><h4>Subsetting a usage via the Textual Editor</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To subset a usage via the Textual Editor</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, identify the usage element that you want to be the subsetting one.</li><li>Follow the element name with either one of the following:<ol><li>keyword<em> subsets</em></li><li>textual symbol <em>:&gt;</em></li></ol></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Follow the keyword with the name of the subsetted usage element. </p></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="77fd9c51-99a2-4270-b869-91211c89a974"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">engine<span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">{</span><br style="text-align: left;" /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">   part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">valves</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br style="text-align: left;" /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span><br style="text-align: left;" /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">dieselEngine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span>:&gt; </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">engine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">/* or */<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">dieselEngine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span>subsets</span><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">engine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Subsetting a usage via the Drag and Drop handler in a view</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To subset a usage via the Drag and Drop handler in a view</span></p><hr /><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's<span> </span></span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Drag and Drop handler" /></ac:link> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">on an <span>element symbol executing </span></span>the <strong>Set Subsetted Feature</strong><strong> </strong>command.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="45a79e26-2c1a-4b93-8ebd-e2e99edf7e4f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To create a new element with the subsetting relationship between the source and target, use the <strong>Create Subsetting Usage </strong>command.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Subsetting a usage via the view palette</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To subset a usage via the view palette</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Specializations group, select <strong>subsetting</strong>. </li><li>Draw the line with the arrowhead going from the subsetting usage to the subsetted one.<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Subsetting.png" /></ac:image></span></li></ol><h4>Subsetting a usage <span>via the Create Relation command</span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To subset a usage </span>via the Create Relation command</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the element that you want to be the subsetting one.</li><li>In the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Relation</strong>, then in the submenu, under <strong>Outgoing</strong>, click <strong>subsetting</strong>. The <strong>Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog opens.</li><li>In the <strong>Create New...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, do one of the following:<ol><li>In a view, click the element symbol you want to subset. Hold Shift to select multiple elements. </li><li>Click the <strong>Add <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong>button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to subset and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Drag and drop the needed element you want to subset from the Containment tree onto the <strong>Create... </strong>dialog.</li></ol></li><li>In the <strong>Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, click <strong>OK</strong>. </li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577662 space=SYSML2P version=7 -->
## PAGE 00709: Succession

- page_id: `249577662`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577662/Succession
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-12-11T20:05:51.322+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A succession is a binary connection that requires its two related usages to have values that are occurrences that happen completely separated in time. A succession asserts that the first action must complete before the second can begin. Successions can be used to assert the ordering of any kind of occurrences in time, but are particularly useful for event occurrences and performances of actions.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The image displays succession connectors, where one action succeeds another.Additionally, the *startTrip*succession connector is also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''].

##### Creating succession usage elements

###### Creating succession usage elements via the Textual Editor

To create succession usage elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the succession keyword.
2. Specify the element name (optional), [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional), then the keyword first , followed by the name of the first element you want to connect via a succession , then the keyword then , followed by the name of the second element you want to connect. If the succession has no name and no specialization, the keyword *succession*can be omitted.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

50%

// The two related features of a succession are identified after its declaration part and before its body,following the keyword *succession*and separated by the keyword *then*.Actions can be declared separately from the successions:

actiontransportPassenger{ 
actionenterVehicle;// action declarations 
actiondriveVehicle; 
actionstopVehicle; 
actionexitVehicle;

successionstartTripfirstenterVehiclethendriveVehicle;// named successions with source (*first*) and target (*then*) 
successionexecuteTripfirstdriveVehiclethenstopVehicle; 
successionfinishTripfirststopVehiclethenexitVehicle;

// OR

// The keyword *succession* may be omitted for unnamed successions:

firstenterVehiclethendriveVehicle;// unnamed successions with source (*first*) and target (*then*) 
firstdriveVehiclethenstopVehicle; 
firststopVehiclethenexitVehicle; 
}

50%

// Action declarations can be combined with successions:

actiontransportPassenger{ 
actionenterVehicle;// action declaration 
thenactiondriveVehicle;// unnamed successions (*then*) with action declarations (*action <action_name>*) 
thenactionstopVehicle; 
thenactionexitVehicle; 
}

// If a succession is placed lexically directly between the two occurrence usages that are its related elements, then the declaration of the succession can be shortened to just the keyword *then*, prepended to the declaration of the second occurrence usage:

actiontransportPassenger{ 
actionenterVehicle;// action declaration 
firstenterVehiclethendriveVehicle;// unnamed succession with source (*first*) and target (*then*) 
actiondriveVehicle;// action declaration 
firstdriveVehiclethenstopVehicle; 
actionstopVehicle; 
firststopVehiclethenexitVehicle; 
actionexitVehicle; 
}

#### NOTE: Succession shorthands for actions

In addition to the regular full notation for successions, you can also use textual notation succession shorthands within the body of an action definition or usage.

#### EXPAND: Succession shorthands for actions

Succession shorthands for actions

// The source and target actions can be specified separately (first ...; then...;) instead of a single single succession (first ... then...;):

actiontransportPassenger{ 
actionenterVehicle;// action declarations 
actiondriveVehicle; 
actionstopVehicle; 
actionexitVehicle;

firstenterVehicle; 
thendriveVehicle;// unnamed successions 
firstdriveVehicle; 
thenstopVehicle; 
firststopVehicle; 
thenexitVehicle; 
}

// The *then* keyword may also be followed by a complete action usage declaration if source and target actions are specified separately:

actiontransportPassenger{ 
actionenterVehicle; 
firstenterVehicle; 
thenactiondriveVehicle; 
firstdriveVehicle; 
thenactionstopVehicle; 
firststopVehicle; 
thenactionexitVehicle; 
}

// The action preceding the *then* keyword is the source of the succession:

actiontransportPassenger{ 
actionenterVehicle; 
firstenterVehicle; 
thenactiondriveVehicle; 
thenactionstopVehicle; 
thenactionexitVehicle; 
}

###### Creating succession usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view

To create succession usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view

1. In a view, select an element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the succession command.
2. Click on the element you want to connect via a succession. A succession path is created between the elements.

###### Creating succession usage elements via the view palette

To create succession usage elements via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the **succession** button to create a succession usage.
2. In a view, click on the first of the two elements you want to connect via a succession, then on the second. A succession is created between the elements.

###### Creating succession usage elements via the Create Relation command

To create succession usage elements via the Create Relation command

1. Right-click one of the elements you want to connect via a succession.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relation , then in the submenu, under Outgoing , click succession . The Create... dialog opens.
3. In the Create... dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to connect. Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to connect and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to connect from the Containment tree onto the Create... dialog.
4. In the Create... dialog, click OK .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">enterVehicle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;  </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// action declaration</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">first</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h4>Creating succession usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view</h4><p>To create succession usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, select an element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the <strong>succession </strong>command.</li><li>Click on the element you want to connect via a succession. A succession path is created between the elements.</li></ol><h4>Creating succession usage elements via the view palette</h4><p>To create succession usage elements via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the<span> <strong>succession </strong></span><span>button to create a succession usage.</span></li><li>In a view, click on the first of the two elements you want to connect via a succession, then on the second. A succession is created between the elements.</li></ol><h4>Creating succession usage elements <span>via the Create Relation command</span></h4><p>To create succession usage elements via the Create Relation command</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click one of the elements you want to connect via a succession.</li><li>In the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Relation</strong>, then in the submenu, under <strong>Outgoing</strong>, click <strong>succession</strong>. The <strong>Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog opens.</li><li>In the <strong>Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, do one of the following:<ol><li>In a view, click the element symbol you want to connect. Hold Shift to select multiple elements. </li><li>Click the <strong>Add <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong>button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to connect and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Drag and drop the needed element you want to connect from the Containment tree onto the <strong>Create... </strong>dialog.</li></ol></li><li>In the <strong>Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, click <strong>OK</strong>. </li></ol><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577672 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00710: Succession flow

- page_id: `249577672`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577672/Succession+flow
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-17T15:42:53.181+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A succession flow is a transfer that only occurs after the source action ends and before the target action starts. A succession flow connection is specified in the same way as a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow' space=''], but it adds the further constraint that the transfer source must complete before the transfer starts, and the transfer must complete before the target can start.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The image displays the *lightFlow* succession flow connector defined by *LightFlow*from *produceDirectedLight.light*to*reflectLight.light***with the payload *light*typed by *Light*.Additionally, the connector is also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''].

##### Creating succession flow usage elements

###### Creating succession flow usage elements via the Textual Editor

To create succession flow usage elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the succession flow keyword.
2. Specify the element name (optional), [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional), (optional), then the from keyword and the name of the source element, followed by the keyword to and the name of the target element . If the succession flow has no name and no specialization, the keyword *from*can be omitted.
3. Click the Synchronize button. successionflowlightFlow:LightFlowfromproduceDirectedLight.lighttoreflectLight.light; // named and specializedsuccession flowsuccessionflow:LightFlowfromproduceDirectedLight.lighttoreflectLight.light; // unnamed specializedsuccession flowsuccessionflowlightFlowfromproduceDirectedLight.lighttoreflectLight.light; // named unspecializedsuccession flowsuccessionflowproduceDirectedLight.lighttoreflectLight.light; // unnamed unspecializedflow can omit the keyword *from*

###### Creating succession flow usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view

To create succession flow usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view

1. In a view, select an element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the succession flow command.
2. Click on the element you want to connect via a succession flow. A succession flow path is created between the elements.

###### Creating succession flow usage elements via the view palette

To create succession flow usage elements via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the **succession flow** button to create a succession flow usage.
2. In a view, click on the source element. A dropdown menu appears. Select the output element in the menu.
3. Then click on the target element. A dropdown menu appears. Select the compatible input element in the menu. A flow usage is created between the elements.

##### Setting the payload for the flow

While the payload name and sign are displayed on the flow in a view by default, you can hide them using the **Show Payload** [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space=''].

###### Setting the payload for the succession flow via the Textual Editor

To set the payload for the succession flow via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor before the keyword *from*within the succession flow for which you want to set a payload.
2. Declare the keyword of followed by either of the following:
  1. The name of the payload specialized by a definition element.
  2. The name of the definition element you want to set as payload for the flow.
3. Click the Synchronize button. successionflow:LightFlowofLightfromproduceDirectedLight.lighttoreflectLight.light; // specialized(feature typing) succession flow with payload set as a definition elementsuccession flow lightFlow : LightFlow of light : Light from produceDirectedLight.light to reflectLight.light; // specialized(feature typing) succession flow with a named and specialized (feature typed) payload

###### Setting the payload for the succession flow via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To set the payload for the succession flow via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop a definition element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's on a succession flow element symbol executing the Set Payload command.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A succession flow is a transfer that only occurs after the source action ends and before the target action starts. A succession flow connection is specified in the same way as a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[flow]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, but it adds the further constraint that the transfer source must complete before the transfer starts, and the transfer must complete before the target can start.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image flow usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, select an element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the <strong>succession flow </strong>command.</li><li>Click on the element you want to connect via a succession flow. A succession flow path is created between the elements.</li></ol><h4>Creating succession flow usage elements via the view palette</h4><p>To create succession flow usage elements via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the<span> <strong>succession flow </strong></span><span>button to create a succession flow usage.</span></li><li>In a view, click on the source element. A dropdown menu appears. Select the output element in the menu.</li><li>Then click on the target element. A dropdown menu appears. Select the compatible input element in the menu. A flow usage is created between the elements.</li></ol><h3>Setting the payload for the flow</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8179d40d-65ed-4e89-a984-85c94ac2ccde"><ac:rich-text-body><p>While the payload name and sign are displayed on the flow in a view by default, you can hide them using the <strong>Show style]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Setting the payload for the succession flow via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To set the payload for the succession flow via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Textual Editor, place the cursor before the keyword <em>from </em>within the succession flow for which you want to set a payload.</p></li><li>Declare the keyword <em>of<span> </span></em>followed by either of the following:<ol><li>The name of the payload specialized by a definition element. </li><li>The name of the definition element you want to set as payload for the flow. </li></ol></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577564 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
````
## PAGE 00711: Swimlane grid

- page_id: `249577564`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577564/Swimlane+grid
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-17T15:38:06.436+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The swimlane grid symbol allows you to model and organize actions based on the elements responsible for performing those actions.Each swimlane in the swimlane grid representsthe system element responsible for performing actions displayed in the corresponding swimlane. The represented elements are easily identifiable as their names are indicated in the headers of each swimlane with the keyword «performer», indicating their role of beingresponsible for performing actions displayed in their corresponding swimlanes.

The swimlane grid symbol is comprised of two sections:

- The swimlane headers display each swimlane referent's name, i.e., the names of the elements responsible for performing actions displayed in the specific swimlanes.
- The swimlane pool is comprised of individual swimlanes . The swimlane pool allows you to easily model actions and their sequencing based on their performers, i.e., the elements that perform these actions, displayed as separate swimlanes.
  - The individual swimlanes display actions performed by the swimlane referents whose names are indicated in the swimlane headers with the keyword «performer» .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The swimlane grid symbol can be displayed for the following elements:

- perform action . Its swimlanes represent the **subparts** of the part that owns and/or inherits the perform action. The perform action element symbol owns the swimlane grid symbol.
  - For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modeling actions with swimlanes' space=''] page.
- include use case . Its swimlanes represent the subject and actor parameters owned and/or inherited by the include use case. The include use case element symbol owns the swimlane grid symbol.
  - For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use case scenario' space=''] page.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Examples of the swimlane grid symbol in include use case and perform action element symbols. 
The *find person in distress* include use case's subject and actors are represented by the swimlanes. 
The *drone in any mission* part's subparts are represented by the swimlanes displayed for the perform action owned by the containing part.

##### Working with the swimlane grid

To perform the following procedures, the perform action or the include use case that contains the actions (or would contain if they are not created yet)performed by swimlane referents must be displayed in the view.

###### Displaying swimlane grid and creating additional swimlanes

To display swimlane grid and create additional swimlanes

- To display the swimlane grid:
  - Right-click the perform action/include use case symbol and in the shortcut menu, click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] , then Display Swimlanes . The swimlane grid is displayed with swimlanes (along with any actions and connectors that may already be defined in the model), representing elements owned or inherited by the element that owns or inherits the perform action/include use case.
- To create additional swimlanes:
  - Select the header of an existing swimlane and click the + button on the left or the right side of the swimlane, to create a swimlane to the left or the right of the existing one. Creating a new swimlane creates:
    - a subpart for the element that owns or inherits the perform action, represented by the new swimlane.
    - an actor for the include use case, represented by the new swimlane. However, no related subpart is created for the part that owns or inherits the include use case.

###### Displaying actions in swimlanes

To display actions in swimlanes

- Select one swimlane by right-clicking its header or all swimlanes by right-clicking the swimlane pool. In the shortcut menu, click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='']**Display** , then Display Actions in the dropdown menu . The specific swimlane's shortcut menu contains the same commands as the element symbol it represents.

###### Displaying parameters, connectors, or other features for element symbols in swimlanes

To display parameters, connectors, or other features for element symbols in swimlanes

- Right-click the element symbol(s) in the swimlane(s). In the shortcut menu, click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='']**Display** , then click the appropriate command in its dropdown menu.

###### Rearranging swimlanes

To rearrange swimlanes

- Click the header of a swimlane, then the displayed control arrows to move the swimlane left or right. [ATTACHMENT filename='Rearranging_Swimlanes.png']

###### Moving the swimlane grid symbol

To move the swimlane grid symbol

1. Click anywhere on the swimlane pool. The **Move** icon at the top left of the swimlane grid symbol appears.
2. Click the Move icon and move the swimlane grid symbol. [ATTACHMENT filename='Moving_Swimlanes.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=306282609 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00712: Switching between the original used project and its subsequent imports

- page_id: `306282609`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/306282609/Switching+between+the+original+used+project+and+its+subsequent+imports
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-05-08T13:15:46.972+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Project management > Working with projects > Managing local used projects
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In addition to versions, used projects may also have multiple imports of the same project. For example, you might [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing projects in the local repository' space=''] a project that is used in the main project from the local repository to your file system, share it with a colleague who makes changes while you continue working on the main project, and then later [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing projects in the local repository' space=''] your colleague’s modified copy back into your local repository. In this case, the local repository will display both projects: the originally used project and the subsequent import of the same project. For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing project imports in the local repository' space=''] page.

Although the main project continues to use the specific project (and its version) that was originally used, you can switch the used project to its different import if needed. Both the original and the import can be modified and saved as a new version, allowing you to update the used project to its latest version (as described in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Updating used projects to their latest versions' space=''] page).

You can view details about the used project (original or its imports) in the **Project Usages**and the **Advanced Project Usages Configuration**dialogs:

- If the used project was created in the local repository, it is displayed only by indicating the project name and version ( Latest/Oldest Version ).
- If the used project was imported into the local repository, it is displayed using the following structure: <[<file_name>.mdszip imported @<date and time of the import>] <version (Latest/Oldest Version)>> .

To ensure the used project import and/or version information displayed in the Containment tree and the rest of the tool is up to date, press **F5**or click **View**> **Refresh** in the main menu.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Switching between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Containment tree

The Containment tree only displays the project version information (Latest/Oldest). You can view more details about the import in the **Project Usages**and the **Advanced Project Usages Configuration**dialogs.

To switch between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Containment tree

1. In the Containment tree, expand the Used Projects node, then right-click a used project. In the shortcut menu, click Switch To.. . The Switch To menu opens.
2. In the Switch To menu, select the project you want to use. The used project is updated. [ATTACHMENT filename='Switching to a different instance of a used project via the Containment tree.png']

###### Switching between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Project Usages dialog

To switch between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Project Usages dialog

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing local used projects' space='']Open the **Project Usages**dialog and d o one of the following:
  1. In the dialog's toolbar, click the **Change Version** [ATTACHMENT filename='change_version_button.png'] button , and in its dropdown menu, click **Switch To...** . The Switch To menu opens.
  2. In the dialog's project usages tree, right-click a used project and in the shortcut menu, click the **Change Version >** **Switch To...******command. The Switch To menu opens.
2. In the Switch To menu, select the project you want to use.
3. Click the OK button to close the Project Usages dialog. The used project is updated.

###### Switching between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Advanced Project Usages Configuration dialog

To switch between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Advanced Project Usages Configuration dialog

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing local used projects' space='']Open the **Advanced Project Usages Configuration**dialog .
2. In the Advanced Project Usages Configuration dialog, do one of the following:
  1. S elect a used project and in the dialog's toolbar, click the **Change Version****[IMAGE alt='' src='']**button, then in its submenu, click **Switch To...** . The Switch To menu opens.
  2. Right-click a used project and in the shortcut menu, click the **Change Version** command , then in its submenu, click **Switch To...** . The Switch To menu opens.
3. In the Switch To menu, select the project you want to use. The cell in the Action column for the removed used project states ' Switch to <project version> <project import> '. [ATTACHMENT filename='Switching to a different instance of a used project via the Advanced Usages Configuration dialog.png']
4. (Optional) To undo the action, do one of the following:
  1. In the dialog's toolbar, click the Reset Changes for Selection [ATTACHMENT filename='reset_changes_for_selection_button.png'] button.
  2. Right-click the used project and in the shortcut menu, click the Reset Changes for Selection command.
5. Do one of the following:
  1. To save changes and keep the dialog open, click the Apply button.
  2. To save changes and close the dialog, click the OK button.
  3. To discard changes and close the dialog, click the Cancel button.
6. Click the OK button to close the Project Usages dialog.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In addition to versions, used projects may also have multiple imports of the same project. <span>For example, you might <ac:link ac:anchor="Exporting projects from the local repository as .mdszip files"><ri:page ri:content-title="Managing projects in the local repository" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[export]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> a project that is used in the main project from the local repository to your file system, share it with a colleague who makes changes while you continue working on the main project, and then later <ac:link ac:anchor="Importing projects to the local repository"><ri:page ri:content-title="Managing projects in the local repository" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[import]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> your colleague’s modified copy back into your local repository. In this case, the local repository will display both projects: the originally used project and the subsequent import of the same project. For more information, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Managing project imports in the local repository" /></ac:link> page.</span></p><p>Although the main project continues to use the specific project (and its version) that was originally used, you can switch the used project to its different import if needed. Both the original and the import can be modified and saved as a new version, allowing you to update the used project to its latest version (as described in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Updating used projects to their latest versions" /></ac:link> page).</p><p>You can view details about the used project (original or its imports) in the <strong>Project Usages </strong>and the <strong>Advanced Project Usages Configuration </strong>dialogs:</p><ul><li data-uuid="caa73de5-cecb-4e46-a0d1-66b9d2c4ad32">If the used project was created in the local repository, it is displayed only by indicating the project name and version (<em>Latest/Oldest Version</em>). </li><li data-uuid="d21cca26-d712-4a15-97bf-7522917126ab">If the used project was imported into the local repository, it is displayed using the following structure: <em>&lt;[&lt;file_name&gt;.mdszip imported @&lt;date and time of the import&gt;] &lt;version (Latest/Oldest Version)&gt;&gt;</em>.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="90219e0c-2b07-4bda-93e1-f8846b407d44"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To ensure the used project import and/or version information displayed in the Containment tree and the rest of the tool is up to date, press <strong>F5 </strong>or click <strong>View </strong>&gt; <strong>Refresh</strong> in the main menu.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Switching to a different revision of a used project.png" /></ac:image></p><h4>Switching between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Containment tree </h4><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2a60eb6c-e308-4df6-b7c3-a12ef1425039"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Containment tree only displays the project version information (Latest/Oldest). You can view more details about the import in the <strong>Project Usages </strong>and the <strong>Advanced Project Usages Configuration </strong>dialogs.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To switch between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Containment tree</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="b76f6ccf-a757-4668-9535-ba36a94b598d">In the Containment tree, expand the<span> </span><strong>Used Projects</strong><span> </span>node, then right-click a used project. In the shortcut menu, click <strong>Switch To..</strong>. The <strong>Switch To </strong>menu opens.</li><li data-uuid="fafab55b-96d1-4435-92ca-062a7dd90d51">In the <strong>Switch To </strong>menu, select the project you want to use.<br />The used project is updated.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Switching to a different instance of a used project via the Containment tree.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><h4>Switching between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Project Usages dialog</h4><p>To switch between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Project Usages dialog</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="734fa276-6ddc-4c47-9b62-6fc7320b493f"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Project Usages dialog"><ri:page ri:content-title="Managing local used projects" /><ac:link-body>Open the <strong>Project Usages </strong>dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> and d<span>o one of the following:</span><ol><li><span>In the dialog's toolbar, click the <strong>Change Version </strong></span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="change_version_button.png" /></ac:image><span> button</span>, and in its dropdown menu, click <span><strong>Switch To...</strong></span><span>. The </span><strong>Switch To </strong>menu opens.</li><li><span>In the dialog's project usages tree, right-click a used project and in the shortcut menu, click the <strong>Change Version &gt;</strong> <strong>Switch To...</strong><strong> </strong>command. </span><span>The </span><strong>Switch To </strong>menu opens.</li></ol></li><li data-uuid="4c5b9af8-018f-438f-8672-2c9f61bc6019">In the <strong>Switch To </strong>menu, select the project you want to use.</li><li data-uuid="de1778a0-3463-44b8-a635-d01a97a9159f">Click the <strong>OK </strong>button to close the <strong>Project Usages </strong>dialog.<br />The used project is updated.</li></ol><h4>Switching between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Advanced Project Usages Configuration dialog</h4><p>To switch between the original used project and its subsequent imports via the Advanced Project Usages Configuration dialog</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="a8d8e603-935b-4320-a5b1-b514b5d93225"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Advanced Project Usages Configuration dialog"><ri:page ri:content-title="Managing local used projects" /><ac:link-body>Open the <strong>Advanced Project Usages Configuration </strong>dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</li><li data-uuid="60409c82-500f-4cca-8fd7-f7bfcdf1d924">In the <strong>Advanced Project Usages Configuration</strong> dialog, do one of the following:<ol><li>S<span>elect a used project and in the dialog's toolbar, click the <strong>Change Version</strong><strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="change_version_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong>button, then in its submenu, click <strong>Switch To...</strong></span><span>. The </span><strong>Switch To </strong>menu opens.</li><li>Right-click <span>a used project and in the shortcut menu, click the <strong>Change Version</strong> command</span><span>, then in its submenu, click <strong>Switch To...</strong></span><span>. </span><span>The </span><strong>Switch To </strong>menu opens.</li></ol></li><li>In the <strong>Switch To </strong>menu, select the project you want to use.<span><br /></span>The cell in the <strong>Action</strong> column for the removed used project states '<strong>Switch to &lt;project version&gt; &lt;project import&gt;</strong>'.  <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Switching to a different instance of a used project via the Advanced Usages Configuration dialog.png" /></ac:image></li><li data-uuid="4da4a7b6-1046-4bd6-bf11-5841b58dbe92">(Optional) To undo the action, do one of the following:<ol><li>In the dialog's toolbar, click the <strong>Reset Changes for Selection</strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="reset_changes_for_selection_button.png" /></ac:image> button.</li><li>Right-click the used project and in the shortcut menu, click the <strong>Reset Changes for Selection </strong>command.</li></ol></li><li data-uuid="e2d79801-9308-4d57-9ebd-4258e43dc6ac">Do one of the following:<ol><li>To save changes and keep the dialog open, click the <strong>Apply </strong>button.</li><li>To save changes and close the dialog, click the <strong>OK </strong>button.</li><li>To discard changes and close the dialog, click the <strong>Cancel</strong> button.</li></ol></li><li data-uuid="00efa913-4221-4fc8-bae0-425900dddf33">Click the <strong>OK </strong>button to close the <strong>Project Usages </strong>dialog.</li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576729 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00713: Switching to System Engineer SysML v2 perspective

- page_id: `249576729`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576729/Switching+to+System+Engineer+SysML+v2+perspective
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-11-27T09:22:30.577+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Project management > Modeling tool environment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When working with SysML v2 features, you should switch the modeling tool's perspective to the System Engineer SysML v2 perspective.

Generally, when you create a new SysML v2 project, a question dialog is prompted, asking you to switch the perspective. Clicking Yes changes the modeling tool's perspective to the System Engineer SysML v2 perspective.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Otherwise, see the procedures below on how to switch the perspective.

##### Switching to the System Engineer SysML v2 perspective

To switch to the System Engineer SysML v2 perspective

- Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select**Options**>**Perspectives**>**Perspectives**. In the**Select Perspective**dialog, select the**System Engineer SysML v2**perspective, and click the**Apply**button.
  - In the main toolbar, click the**Perspective**drop-down box and select the**System Engineer SysML v2**perspective.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>When working with SysML v2 features, you should switch the modeling tool's perspective to the System Engineer SysML v2 perspective. </p><p>Generally, when you create a new SysML v2 project, a question dialog is prompted, asking you to switch the perspective. Clicking Yes changes the modeling tool's perspective to the System Engineer SysML v2 perspective.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="question_dialog.png" /></ac:image></p><p>Otherwise, see the procedures below on how to switch the perspective.</p><h3>Switching to the System Engineer SysML v2 perspective</h3><p>To switch to the System Engineer SysML v2 perspective</p><hr /><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Do one of the following:</span></span><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In the main menu, select<span> </span><strong>Options<span> </span></strong>&gt;<span> </span><strong>Perspectives<span> </span></strong>&gt;<span> </span><strong>Perspectives</strong>. In the<span> </span><strong>Select Perspective</strong><span> </span>dialog, select the<span> </span><strong>System Engineer SysML v2</strong><span> </span>perspective, and click the<span> </span><strong>Apply<span> </span></strong>button.</span></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In the main toolbar, click the<span> </span><strong>Perspective</strong><span> </span>drop-down box and select the<span> </span><strong>System Engineer SysML v2</strong><span> </span>perspective.</span></span></li></ul></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577838 space=SYSML2P version=9 -->
## PAGE 00714: Symbol styles

- page_id: `249577838`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577838/Symbol+styles
- version_number: 9
- version_date: `2026-05-22T10:37:08.978+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Views
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Element symbols, including shapes and paths, are displayed in views based on the symbol styles defined in the style sheets in the *3DS SysML Customization*library that is loaded in every project. You can modify element symbol styles according to your preferences by working with style sheets.

Types of style sheet specification:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Predefined style sheets' space=''] Each SysML v2 project is automatically loaded with the 3DS SysML Customization library containing predefined style sheets. Each style sheet contains symbol style rules based on the element metaclasses. The *DefaultStyleSheet*style sheet contains model-based styles that apply to all element symbols in SysML v2 projects by default based on element metaclass.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom style sheets' space=''] : Instead of or in addition to using the existing style sheets, you can also create custom style sheets with your own sets of rules for symbol styles.
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extracting style sheets' space=''] In a view displaying elements with applied metadata, you can automatically extract a style sheet based on the applied metadata, applied metadata feature value, specialized library element, or library element feature value. The extracted style sheet is automatically applied to the view and the displayed symbols.
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating style sheets' space=''] Create a style sheet with your own custom rules.
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating style sheets' space=''] Create custom rules and customize the rules inherited from a reused style sheet.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space=''] Whether using a default or a custom style, y ou can customize the styles of individual symbols. Using the Symbol Style dialog, you can specify the style properties for specific symbols, determining their appearance. Changing the styles for an individual symbol via the Symbol Style dialog overwrites predefined and custom model-based symbol styles; the modifications persist even after updating [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space=''] .
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual views' space=''] Whether using a default or a custom style, y ou can customize the styles of individual views. Using the View Style dialog, you can specify the style properties for specific views, determining their appearance. Changing the styles for an individual view via the View Style dialog overwrites predefined and custom model-based symbol styles; the modifications persist even after updating [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space=''] .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### On the left: symbol styles using a predefined style sheet *DefaultStyleSheet*. 
In the middle: symbol styles using a predefined style sheet*DefaultStyleSheet*with the customized style for the individual attribute symbol. 
On the right: symbol styles using a custom style sheet.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577012 space=SYSML2P version=15 -->
## PAGE 00715: Symbolic views

- page_id: `249577012`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577012/Symbolic+views
- version_number: 15
- version_date: `2026-07-01T12:08:20.198+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Views > Creating views
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Symbolic views allow you to model via symbolic model representation.In a symbolic view, elements are represented as shapes, and relationships and connectors as paths, both shapes and paths are referred to as symbols.Youcan use symbolic views for modeling and representing parts interconnection, action flow, requirements, etc.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The image displays a standard predefined symbolic view in the View Editor tab with the toolbar, pane, and palette. Elements and connectors are displayed as shapes and paths on the pane.

##### Symbolic views

Specific symbolic views you create in the modeling tool are predefined symbolic view usages defined by appropriate predefined symbolic view definitions. These predefined symbolic view definitions can differ in the palettes they provide, renderings and/or element filters they specify.

- For symbolic view creation procedures, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating symbolic views' space=''] page.
- Additionally, you can create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom symbolic view definitions' space=''] .

Available symbolic view definitions are differentiated as follows:

- **Standard predefined symbolic view definitions**
  - Do not have any specified filters, meaning, any elements can be exposed by these views. Once you have created such a view, you may want to specify [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Filtering elements for views' space=''] to limit the displayed elements.
  - Can be created via the Textual Editor or the Create View command in the Containment tree.
- Predefined symbolic view definitions that use expressions
  - Contain element filters specified in the*DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression**::NonStandardLibraryElementFilter*and *DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression**::***EssentialElementsFilter**view definitions, narrowing down what elements can be exposed by the view by discarding various standard library elements and other non-essential elements that are unlikely to be relevant for display in views.
  - Additionally, most of the predefined symbolic view definitions that use expressions contain additional filters to narrow down the exposed elements, e.g., *PartsTreeView*view definition contains filters specifying that only part usage elements can be exposed.
  - Can be created only via the Textual Editor.

See the table below listing all predefined symbolic view definitions.

| View definitions | Description | Palette | Availability |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Standard predefined symbolic view definitions |  |  |  |
| General View | Standard predefined multi-purpose general symbolic view for element representation and modeling. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. | Via Textual EditorVia the Create View command |
| Interconnection View | Standard predefined symbolic view for element interconnection representation and modeling. | Includes a view palette with button categories optimized for interconnections modeling. |  |
| Action Flow View | Standard predefined symbolic view for action flow representation and modeling.>]]> | Includes a view palette with button categories optimized for action flow modeling. |  |
| State Transition View | Standard predefined symbolic view for state transition representation and modeling.>]]> | Includes a view palette with button categories optimized for state transition modeling. |  |
| Sequence View | Standard predefined symbolic view for representing and modeling messages with time-ordered event occurrences on lifelines of exposed features.>]]>Sequence View is provided as a technology preview. Reordering messages in the view does not update their order in the model. Message order is refreshed from the model during Display and Layout actions. | Includes a view palette with button categories optimized for sequence modeling. |  |
| Predefined symbolic view definitions that use expressions |  |  |  |
| TreeView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display common elements in tree-view rendering style. It subclassifies the NonStandardLibraryElementFilter and EssentialElementsFilter view definitions, which include filters to exclude various standard library elements and other non-essential elements that are unlikely to be relevant for display in views. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. | Via Textual Editor |
| NestedView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display common elements in nested-view rendering style.It subclassifies the NonStandardLibraryElementFilter and EssentialElementsFilter view definitions, which include filters to exclude various standard library elements and other non-essential elements that are unlikely to be relevant for display in views. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. |  |
| UsagesNestedView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display usage elements in nested-view rendering style.It subclassifies the NestedView view definition. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. |  |
| PartsNestedView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display parts as nested shapes, with the connections between them represented as paths.It subclassifies the UsagesNestedView view definition. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. |  |
| Parts&PortsNestedView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display parts and ports as nested shapes, with the connections between them represented as paths.It subclassifies the UsagesNestedView view definition. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. |  |
| PartsTreeView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display parts in a decomposition tree style.It subclassifies the TreeView view definition. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. |  |
| ActionsNestedView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display actions as nested shapes, parameters as shapes on border, and in-between connections represented as paths.It subclassifies the UsagesNestedView view definition. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. |  |
| ActionsTreeView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display actions in a decomposition tree style. It subclassifies the TreeView view definition. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. |  |
| RequirementTreeView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display the exposed requirements in a decomposition tree style. It subclassifies the TreeView view definition. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. |  |
| StatesNestedView | Predefined symbolic view definition that uses expressions to display states as nested shapes, with transitions between them represented as paths.It subclassifies the UsagesNestedView view definition. | Includes a view palette with all available button categories. |  |

##### Symbolic view areas

Views are displayed in the View Editor tab.

- Toolbar allows you to quickly access the options for managing element symbols in the view. See the subsection.
- Pane allows you to model and represent elements as symbols. For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] page.
- Palette allows you to model elements. See the subsection.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Symbolic view palette

The palette contains buttons for the majority of the elements you can create in the view pane, as well as additional buttons for working with symbols in the view pane.

- The buttons are ordered into categories: Common, Items/Ports/Attributes, Connectors, Specializations, Other Relationships, Actions, Other Actions, States, Cases, Requirements/Constraints. Click the category title to expand the contained element buttons.
- Some element buttons have a down-facing arrow button allowing you to view the button's submenu of other element kinds (e.g., part and part def or in ref , in item , and in attribute ).
- The available buttons and their categories depend on the symbolic view definition.
- You can create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom symbolic view definitions' space=''] .

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']View palette's Items/Ports/Attributes category with the *part*button's submenu.

###### Symbolic view toolbar buttons

The table below shows the buttons available in the symbolic view toolbar.

| Button | Shortcut keys | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Navigation toolbar |  |  |
| Select in Containment tree | Alt+B | Click the button to highlight the element of the selected symbol in the Containment tab of the Model Browser. |
| Layout toolbar |  |  |
| Quick Diagram Layout | Ctrl+Q | Click the arrow button to display the dropdown menu. In it, select the Layout SysML v2 Style option. |
| Align | - | Click the button to display the dropdown menu with the symbol alignment options. |
| Reset Label Positions | - | Click the button to reset the labels' position to default. Make sure the labels are selected first. |
| Edit toolbar |  |  |
| Copy | Ctrl+C | Click to copy the selected symbol. |
| Paste | Ctrl+V | Click to paste the copied symbol. |
| Delete | Ctrl+D | Click to remove selected elements both from the view and the model. |
| Remove From Diagram | Delete | Click to remove the selected element from the view. |
| Shape Editing toolbar |  |  |
| Make Preferred Size | - | Click to resize the selected symbol(s) to the most compact size. |
| Display toolbar |  |  |
| Display | - | Click the arrow button to display the dropdown menu. In that menu, select the kind of symbols you want to display in the view for the selected symbol. You can also choose the style in which the symbols can be displayed, i.e., tree-view or nested. |
| Style Sheets toolbar |  |  |
| Style Sheets | - | Click to activate style sheets for the view or the entire project. >]]> |
| Other buttons |  |  |
| Back | Alt+Left Arrow | Click to navigate to the previously opened view. |
| Forward | Alt+Right Arrow | Click to navigate to the previously opened view. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Symbolic views allow you to model via symbolic model representation. </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">In a symbolic view, elements are represented as shapes, and relationships and connectors as paths, both shapes and paths are referred to as symbols. </span></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">You </span>can use symbolic views for modeling and representing <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">parts interconnection, action flow, requirements, etc</span>. </span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="symbolic_view_example.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The image displays a standard predefined symbolic view in the View Editor tab with the toolbar, pane, and palette. Elements and connectors are displayed as shapes and paths on the pane.  </h6><h3>Symbolic views</h3><p>Specific symbolic views you create in the modeling tool are predefined symbolic view usages defined by appropriate predefined symbolic view definitions. These predefined symbolic view definitions can differ in the palettes they provide, renderings and/or element filters they specify. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2cfe40a7-b52d-49a2-98b4-83b0292b7f7c"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span>For symbolic view creation procedures, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating symbolic views" /></ac:link> page.</span></li><li><span>Additionally, you can create </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Custom symbolic view definitions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[custom symbolic view definitions with customized palettes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Available symbolic view definitions are differentiated as follows:</p><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><strong>Standard predefined symbolic view definitions</strong> </span><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Do not have any specified filters, meaning, any elements can be exposed by these views. Once you have created such a view, you may want to specify <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Filtering elements for views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[filters]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to limit the displayed elements.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Can be created via the Textual Editor or the Create View command in the Containment tree.</span></li></ul></li><li><strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Predefined symbolic view definitions that use expressions</span></strong><ul><li data-uuid="ad2b1ac9-2819-4de9-aacf-e253ec9ca4b1"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Contain element filters specified in the<span> <em>DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression</em></span><em>::NonStandardLibraryElementFilter<span> </span></em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">and <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span><em>DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression</em></span><em>::</em></span></span><em><span><em style="text-align: left;">EssentialElementsFilter </em></span></em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">view definitions</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">, narrowing down what elements can be exposed by the view by discarding various standard library elements and other non-essential elements that are unlikely to be relevant for display in views. </span></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Additionally, most of the predefined symbolic view definitions that use expressions contain additional filters to narrow down the exposed elements, e.g., <em>PartsTreeView<span> </span></em>view definition contains filters specifying that only part usage elements can be exposed.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Can be created only via the Textual Editor.</span></li></ul></li></ul><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">See the table below listing all predefined symbolic view definitions.</span></p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 88.1841%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 14.4564%;" /><col style="width: 53.4014%;" /><col style="width: 20.4383%;" /><col style="width: 11.6523%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td class="highlight-#f4f5f7" style="text-align: center;" data-highlight-colour="#f4f5f7"><strong title=""><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);" title="">View definitions</span></strong></td><td class="highlight-#f4f5f7" style="text-align: center;" data-highlight-colour="#f4f5f7"><strong title=""><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);" title="">Description</span></strong></td><td class="highlight-grey" style="text-align: center;" title="Background color : " data-highlight-colour="grey"><strong title=""><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);" title="">Palette</span></strong></td><td class="highlight-#f4f5f7" style="text-align: center;" data-highlight-colour="#f4f5f7"><strong title=""><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);" title="">Availability</span></strong></td></tr><tr><td class="highlight-#deebff" colspan="4" data-highlight-colour="#deebff"><strong title=""><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);" title="">Standard predefined symbolic view definitions</span></strong></td></tr><tr><td><p>General View</p></td><td><p>Standard predefined multi-purpose general symbolic view for element representation and modeling. </p></td><td><ul><li>Includes a view palette with all available button categories.</li></ul></td><td rowspan="5"><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Via Textual Editor</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Via the Create View command</span></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> </span></td></tr><tr><td><p>Interconnection View </p></td><td><p>Standard predefined symbolic view for element interconnection representation and modeling.</p></td><td><ul><li>Includes a view palette with button categories optimized for interconnections modeling.</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td><p>Action Flow View</p></td><td><p>Standard predefined symbolic view for action flow representation and modeling.</p><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Actions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about modeling actions >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></td><td><ul><li>Includes a view palette with button categories optimized for action flow modeling.</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td><p>State Transition View</p></td><td><p>Standard predefined symbolic view for state transition representation and modeling.</p><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="States" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about modeling states >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></td><td><ul><li>Includes a view palette with button categories optimized for state transition modeling.</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td><p>Sequence View</p></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Standard predefined symbolic view for representing and modeling messages with time-ordered event >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2413ea57-a2d0-4982-a3df-a40a7fbae516"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Sequence View is provided as a technology preview. Reordering messages in the view does not update their order in the model. Message order is refreshed from the model during Display and Layout actions.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></div></td><td><ul><li data-uuid="790c514a-7bee-42e7-a2f0-2c4ef14cf4e7">Includes a view palette with button categories optimized for sequence modeling.</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td class="highlight-#deebff" colspan="4" definition.</li><li>You can create <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Custom symbolic view definitions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[custom symbolic view definitions with customized
````
